cases.title:
here's a full A-Z guide on how you can start a faceless youtube channel in 2026

and how you can avoid getting demonetized even if you're using AI

so everyone been freaking out about the new AI polic
cases.content:
here's a full A-Z guide on how you can start a faceless youtube channel in 2026

and how you can avoid getting demonetized even if you're using AI

so everyone been freaking out about the new AI policies

some people even think youtube automation is "dead"

but after running faceless channels myself and seeing hundreds of students launch channels every day and make life changing money out of it, I can tell you that youtube automation is nowhere near dead

here's what's actually happening:

youtube implemented Google Gemini as their new surveillance system

it's way more sophisticated than before

it scans everything you post and understands if your video is actual value or just slop

this led to a wave of terminations but it's only affecting the lowest level bare minimum AI slop content

not quality AI channels

I had a 300K subscriber channel terminated myself but me and my students are still using AI to create content faster and cheaper without getting hit

the key is we're using AI to help create content faster and more cost-efficiently

we're not 100% relying on AI to do everything for us

here's what's safe right now:

AI script + AI voiceover + AI images or stock footage put together in CapCut

as long as you have compelling visuals and you're providing value, you're fine

don't just show one image for multiple minutes, that's lazy and gets you flagged

also don't post the same video multiple times with slight changes, that's spammy

alright now here's the actual step-by-step process:

step 1: pick your niche

avoid niches flooded with AI slop

look for channels under 100K subs getting 50K-200K+ views consistently

if you see 50 channels posting the same recycled content, that's a red flag

youtube will associate your channel with that spam

step 2: find viral topics

go to your competitor's channel

filter by "most popular"

find their viral outliers (videos with way more views than their average)

copy the topic, not the exact execution

take inspiration but package it differently

step 3: write your script with Claude

this is where most people mess up

here's exactly what to do:

go to your competitor's viral video

use youtubetotranscript .com to grab the transcript

paste it into Claude

use this prompt:

"I'm creating a YouTube video for my [niche] channel. Below is a transcript from a successful video in my niche. I want you to write a NEW script on the topic [your specific topic] that follows the same style, and structure as the reference transcript below. Never use one-to-one copies of sentences from the reference script"

then paste the transcript

Claude will write you a script that matches the style but isn't plagiarized

don't just copy-paste your competitor's script word for word, that gets you terminated

step 4: generate voiceover with ElevenLabs

go to ElevenLabs

pick a voice that fits your niche (calm and authoritative for history, energetic for tech, etc)

don't pick the same voice everyone else in your niche uses

analyze why certain voices work then pick a different voice with those same qualities

paste your script in SMALL CHUNKS (not the whole thing at once)

ElevenLabs makes mistakes on long generations so generate section by section

download the MP3s

step 5: get visuals from Pixabay

Pixabay has free stock footage for any niche

search your keyword (space, finance, history, whatever)

download clips in 1920x1080

build a library of reusable clips you can shuffle in different videos

step 6: edit in CapCut

upload your stock footage clips

add your voiceover

add background music from Pixabay (turn it down to -20dB so it doesn't overpower the voice)

add simple transitions between clips

optionally add auto-captions

export

the whole editing process takes like 30 minutes

now here's the shadowban stuff you need to know:

shadowbans are super common now, basically everyone gets shadowbanned if they do this wrong

best way to avoid it: use your pre-existing channel (the one you watch youtube with)

it has a high trust score cuz you've been using it for years

your Gmail is old, you've watched videos on it, you've liked and commented

youtube trusts it

don't create a fresh Gmail + fresh channel on the same day and immediately start uploading

that screams "spam bot"

also don't change your channel name or profile picture before you start posting

I've seen people get good traction on their first 2-3 videos then change the channel name and the traffic dies

wait until you get consistent traffic first

how to actually get views in 2025:

post longer videos (2-3 hour videos)

youtube wants to keep viewers on the platform as long as possible and they'll reward you for it

psychologically if you start watching a 2-hour video you're more likely to stay longer than a 7-minute video

longer videos also get you 2-3x higher RPM

channels posting 2-3 hour videos are making $5K-$10K per month even without crazy views

upload frequently (daily or multiple times per day)

gives the algorithm more data to find your audience faster

youtube likes active channels

but don't post slop just to post often, you'll get terminated

if one of your videos starts getting traction, don't immediately post another video the next day

let it breathe

the second video can kill the first one's momentum

this applies to new channels, not established ones

also it takes 10-15 uploads now to start getting traction (not 2-3 like before)

most beginners quit after 5-10 videos cuz they don't know this

example: one of my students Gus posted 15-20 videos before seeing any results

stayed consistent, then his channel blew up

went from $0 to $12K in 20 days of monetization

if you're staying consistent and not seeing results, it's not the algorithm

it's your content, topics, packaging, or niche

and that’s it...

save this, and whenever you feel stuck, go back here and read it again…

if you do this, consistently without quitting, then 2026 will be your year.

cases.title:
I make $20k/m profit with YouTube, here are 5 uncomfortable truths I learned the hard way.

if you ignore these you will fail...👇🧵

1. RPM Matters A LOT

You need to deeply understand how RPM's work o
cases.content:
I make $20k/m profit with YouTube, here are 5 uncomfortable truths I learned the hard way.

if you ignore these you will fail...👇🧵

1. RPM Matters A LOT

You need to deeply understand how RPM's work on YouTube, otherwise it's impossible to estimate the earning potential from a niche.

1 million views can look like:
$30 - Shorts
$1000 - Gaming Long Form
$10,000 - Business Long Form

Study this...

2. Systems scale, solo fails

You need to systemize & outsource everything.

The power of YouTube automation is that you can make $10k+ / month while only working a few hours

IF you systemize and outsource effectively.

Sadly most people want to penny pinch and end up overwhelmed. (if you don't outsource you value your time at less than $3/hour lol)

3. Consistency is key

You aren't going to get rich quick with YouTube

It's truly a game of consistency - just like anything.

You need to spend countless hours failing, to improve & master your craft.

This means multiple channels, niches, and videos with little results before you see any kind of traction.

You CANNOT skip this phase, you can only speed it up by learning from others

4. Dont be scared

What if this niche doesn't work? What if YouTube Kills my channel? What if XYZ

I used to worry a lot about these small potential failure points

Until I spoke to my friend about it, who told me these worries will occur in ANY business, and that if he had worried about these things then he wouldn't be a millionaire today

You can't stop small setbacks, but on a big scale they don't matter

5. DO THE WORK

Stop spending hours per day reading twitter for new niches and just get started. 

People spend months, even YEARS, reading and researching to ultimately NEVER get started.

You need to start. You will suck. You will improve.

Scrolling twitter makes you feel productive, but is actually a waste of time - mostly

Final Take:

If you actually consistently stick to YouTube for 1 year, you will see success. Get started, stay consistent. And if all fails, f*ck it - at least you won't forever ask yourself what if....

Student Lars spent 1.5 years with some other YouTube Automation course

Got nowhere & made like $200 total... learned all the wrong strategies

cases.content:
Student Lars spent 1.5 years with some other YouTube Automation course

Got nowhere & made like $200 total... learned all the wrong strategies

Then he joined my program and everything changed

- Monetized in 6 weeks
- $5K/month by week 10
- $10K+ months consistently
- Almost $100K in under a year

Now he quit his job and is heading to Bali for a few months lol

So what actually changed for Lars? 👇🏼

1) He STOPPED trying to reinvent the wheel

Lars was trying to be creative/unique with his videos. I told him to just find what's working and clone it (but better). He started doing that and boom... views came flooding in ;)

2) He focused on ONE channel instead of spreading himself thin

Most people want to start 5 channels at once. Lars went all in on one niche, one format, one strategy until it worked

3) He treated this like a real business

Hired a solid team from the Philippines. $400-500/month per person. Built systems so he could step back. That's when things really scaled

4) He came PREPARED to every call

Asked specific questions & took notes. Actually implemented what we talked about. Most people just show up and expect magic without doing the work

Lars literally told me: I had to forget everything I learned before and think like you

I teach you to keep it stupidly simple and focus on what actually makes money

Lars went through channel terminations and all the bullshit. But he didn't quit

Now he's coaching students himself and getting them results too

His goal for 2026... $50K+ months

And honestly I think he'll hit it

cases.title:
Alright, let's address your points now that I am mostly done with my Thanksgiving day stuff with the family.

First: My Personal Relevancy

Look, I've garnered a following, sure. I have a very small b
cases.content:
Alright, let's address your points now that I am mostly done with my Thanksgiving day stuff with the family.

First: My Personal Relevancy

Look, I've garnered a following, sure. I have a very small bubble of super avid fans/supporters. They seem to like me or whatever - and that's neat. I am very thankful for that, and that they enable me to do something I truly enjoy for a living. Such a luxury in life, to do something you're passionate about and support your children doing it.

I could vanish off the internet tonight, never to be seen again. And while there would be a few dozen wondering what happened, for the most part I'd be forgotten about within a month. Fast forward a year, and people will have forgotten my channel ever existed in the first place. Think about it even for yourself. If I stopped posting on X, stopped making videos and live streams - how long before you literally wouldn't even have a fleeting thought about my existence? I would fade away  into obscurity.

Now, why is this? Well, first what I do is replicable. There are many others who do it, and frankly do it better than I do. But more than that, when news is hot, is when most tune in. When it's not, they don't. Because it's not me they are here for, it's news. Dropped a Sonic Racing video today. Barely crawled to 1,000 views. On a 160k subscriber channel.

This is why I mention that subscriber count means nothing. It CAN to the individual running the show, but ultimately it has nothing to do with viewership and little to do with influence. There are channels with millions of subs you have never heard of, because they only get a few thousand views a video today. Sub count is merely a sign of having high periods.

Most my sub growth came off 3 events. Super Mario 3D All-Stars, Tears of the Kingdom, and the launch of Switch 2. Those 3 time periods make up 130k of my total subscribers. You know what happened after those events passed? A majority stopped watching. Because the thing they subbed to hear about, they no longer need to hear about. Will another game or event give me another explosion in subs one day - maybe. Probably if I am still going. But it doesn't guarantee long term influence or viewership. 

Nintendeen has more views this month than I do, despite publishing less videos and actually being on a viewership decline. Yet he's only at 32k subs. He is INFLUENCING way more people than I am, despite being a small subscribed channel. Because subscribers are not the same as viewership. They are a indicator of prior success, not current. My normal videos this month have 195.5k views across 21 videos. That's about 9.3k views per video.

Nintendeen has 287,100 views so far... over just 11 videos. For an average of 26,000 views per video. Yes, he he has more influence. And that's totally fine.

When I say I am irrelevant - I mean it. Not because there won't be a few dozen people who miss me - but because I don't believe a few dozen hardcore fans = relevancy on the internet. If nothing else, it actually shows how little of a impact I have. Which is fine for me because having an impact isn't why I enjoy doing this. I am legit passionate about talking about video game news. It's enjoyable to me. These back and forths like were having - this is fun to me. But don't confuse just because when I talk publicly on X or YouTube, more people see and hear what I say, as thinking this actually makes me relevant. Relevant people aren't so easily forgotten once they leave. I would be. I don't provide anything of significance to the Nintendo community. I report and cover news. 

And, think about it like this: You claim no one cares what you say, yet not just me, but many others I have seen reply directly right too you over the years. If your stance is "people noticing is what makes you relevant" - you're being noticed. Right now. That is not my stance for relevancy.

You know who I am relevant to? My family. My children. My fiance. My parents and my very best friends. If I die tomorrow, it's not something any of them "just get over" because it's been a year. 10 years. They will still remember me and think about me often. I am very relevant to their lives.

Don't overestimate what online relevancy really is. You are forgotten about faster than you made a name for yourself. That's how the internet works. I have no true relevancy here. I just share opinions and talk about video game news. Some agree, some don't, but I don't have an impact on anything really.

Why You Care:

You claim to care because of my influence. This is dumb. It actually goes to support my stance that the only reason you care about anything I say is because some people give me attention for it. And the moment I stop talking, there is no reason to care anymore, right? Right. Did I ask for Nintendeen to make an entire video just about me? No I did not. Did I ask to be included in Fritanga Plays and OhNoItsAlex videos? No, I did not.

Does me appearing in their content make me relevant? No, it doesn't. Again, if I just stopped - the internet moves on. No one makes decisions because of the things I do. This is what I mean. You care what I say, but do you really? Truly? Or is this is all just entertainment for you that's quickly forgotten the moment I stop responding.

Acting Like I'm More Objective Than I Actually Am

Yes, Princess Peach Showtime sucks. But really, that's all you can think of? How about the fact I publicly blasted Nintendo for lying about their online system change? This was picked up and even covered by Nintendeen and OhNoItsAlex - they used my words to help further their videos take on the situation. Because I was openly and very earnestly blasting Nintendo. 

Where were you when I made a whole video blasting Nintendo of America's treatment of their QA staff? Where were you when I live reacted to Legends ZA, then went on a rant about the DLC offering?

Where were you when I not just casually said I don't like Game Key Cards, but literally put Nintendo on blast for them on the Podcast?

I publicly stated after playing the game, multiple times, that Mario Kart World is not worth $80. No one ever seems to recall this. I blasted them for not including Welcome Tour - even covering Reggie blasting Nintendo for not including it in the box. I blasted Nintendo back in 2018 for lying about what NSO was going to be - as it came out missing a bunch of promised features that we still don't have to this very day.

I've put them on blast not having messaging systems in place, ways to form online lobbies outside of games, and more. I've made multiple videos blasting the eShop and talking about how they need to make it better. There is so much more.

So, why can't you "think" of any of this stuff? Well, you probably haven't watched a majority of my content the last 8 years, for starters. Between videos, live streams, podcasts, and posts online - very few truly could have possibly kept up with all of it. So there is that.

The other is confirmation bias. Once people form an opinion about something, other things get overlooked that don't support the opinion, so you can focus on the things that emphasize it. So you can recall Princess Peach Showtime. But you don't recall this: https://t.co/yIRir2WYN7

Said I don't think Pokopia is worth $70 and that I hate that it's a Key-Card. But since this doesn't support the notion I D-Ride Nintendo, of course it is overlooked.

See, I don't make daily content blasting Nintendo. I don't take one thing I dislike about them, and make the same video on it time and time again on repeat. I don't sit on a soap box and scream from the mountain tops. Because in the end, this is just video games. I made my points already, there is no point to keep making them.

"Brand Loyalty"

I am no more loyal to Nintendo than I am to having some Mountain Dew. The company doesn't mean a whole lot to me. The games, the people, some of the hardware - sure. I have fond memories attached and current modern fun times still happening. But the company is just a business. A for profit entity that makes decisions in the best interest of the long term health and financial success of being a for profit entity.

Unlike many, I don't get pissed at companies being greedy. I expect it. And when your expectations are different than others, things like $70 and $80 games, don't come across as some big surprise worth huffing and puffing until the house falls down kind of news. You either choose to buy the products, or you don't. And the market will set what's allowed.

So, you can call it "toxic positivity" if you like. It's just me expecting greedy companies to be greedy, and looking at the business side of why they make the decisions they do. And the thing is, Nintendo isn't some big evil cog in the middle driving the industry in the long run. They aren't even playing the same game as most of the industry.

You know what cog has driven a worse industry than anything Nintendo has done in the history of the company: Microtransactions.

But I supposed, that's Nintendo's fault too, right? Digital-Only platforms. Inflated game budgets. Day One DLC. 

Let's think of the biggest things people get pissed at Nintendo about today and claim is ruining them and the industry: Game Pricing, Key-Cards, and... I guess console pricing? Maybe the policy to brick systems that literally everyone else has too? I don't know, trying to think of things people can directly point and say is purely Nintendo.

Game Pricing: World ain't the first $80 game. Their standard pricing, as has been proven, is $70. They didn't set that precedent.

Key-Cards. Yeah I hate em. But, is it worse than a code in the box? Is it worse than Indiana Jones on a disc that's just a key-card that triggers a install? All of these things existed, before Key-Cards. 

Console price? It's set by the market it exists in. This ain't apple. They may even be losing money per unit sold in the U.S., at least.

When people talk about how they are ruining themselves and the industry, I always ask why. Not because I am looking to defend them, I am actually just looking to see what the arguments are that are directly Nintendo's fault. And the issues brought up already existed, so I am unsure what I am supposed to be mad at them for overall. Wanting to make money, I guess? Why is this evil? I want to make money too. My sonic video today exists because I was paid to make it. Am I evil?

And this idea that Nintendeen is one of the few people who can say Nintendo is making a lot of mistakes while still loving their games due to retrospectives is just silly.

Has he made a video on a single Nintendo game that released in the last 4 years that's positive? I believe he has a sole video in that span from a game that's actually current, and it was Jamboree. Which he has later made videos also bashing it.

So, he hasn't made positive content really on any game made in the last 4 years. We know he has zero plans to buy a Switch 2, and thus won't be making positive content on Nintendo games in the next 4 years. But, this is a guy that loves Nintendo games but hates Nintendo's business?

Incorrect. This is person who use to enjoy Nintendo games, that no longer does. And once you sap the joy away from the games, you're left with a company making money in ways you don't like, making it easy to call them out. Because the reason they exist is because people enjoy their games. If you stop enjoying them, all you see is dollar signs for things you have little interest in. 

And if you think all that matters is he USE to enjoy Nintendo games - that's fine. That's your subjective stance on what matters. Mine is this: If you aren't partaking in modern Nintendo, you are likely less informed on the ins and outs of modern Nintendo. You are living in the past.

Nintendo is a greedy company. And their relevancy is making games people want to play. So long as they do that, they will find success.

cases.title:
I remember when I first started my agency. For a year, I was looking for some magic strategy that would help me grow…

I never found anything… in fact… the closest thing I have ever come to is these t
cases.content:
I remember when I first started my agency. For a year, I was looking for some magic strategy that would help me grow…

I never found anything… in fact… the closest thing I have ever come to is these two things I do on a daily and weekly basis:

One - YouTube

YouTube has been the biggest unlock for me when it comes to generating new business.

At the time of writing, I post four videos every week across two channels. On one channel, I focus on email marketing strategies; on another channel, I focus on the day-to-day of running my business:

Click here to see my email marketing channel

Click here to see my agency follow-along channel

I am telling you right now, having a YouTube channel, for any business, is one of the best things you can do.

On my email marketing channel, we get about 8k views every month, and we have just shy of 1.1k subs. From that really small audience, I get about 2 - 3 new calls from people every single week.

There is nothing that screams ‘I know what I am talking about’ more than a video literally running people through your strategies and case studies.

It beats LinkedIn posting, Twitter posting, cold emailing, an amazing website… it beats them all.

Now, the 2 - 3 calls per week from YouTube channel are great, but what is also amazing is the trust that I am able to build by having a YouTube channel with people prior to getting on a call with them, who don’t even know I have that channel in the first place.

The authority that sending a video that you recorded about a problem a customer has, prior to getting on a call with them, is insane. It can literally be the difference between closing a deal and losing one.

And it makes sense, right?

Who do you think someone is more likely to work with, the random person from the internet, or the person who has put their face out to the world, showing them the exact solution to the viewer’s problem?

So YouTube is number one.

Also for those people reading this who do not think they have time to make videos… think again.

Keeping in mind that I do not have kids nor a partner right now… I am able to record 4 videos a week, run a team of 10, post on LinkedIn daily and Twitter twice a day, and make two of these newsletters every week, and work across 40 different client accounts.

You have time

Number 2 - Upwork

Everyone thinks that Upwork is only for finding cheap freelancers or crappy jobs.

It’s not.

From Upwork alone, I get on about 4 - 5 calls every single week.

Now, in saying this, my profile is dialled in on that platform. My reviews are well above average, and I am now probably one of the top people on Upwork for email marketing specifically.

But I have worked on that, and it has taken me a long time, but goddamn, does it bear fruit.

Rather than go into my full strategy here, I recommend just checking out my YouTube video I made about this, search @agencyfollowalong on youtube

If you have time, use the strategy above; it will turn your business around.

cases.title: My personal estimate and without any prejudice what Candace Owens made since September 11. this is no judgement about her content and no opinion about her thoughts and assumptions regarding Charlie Ki
cases.content:
My personal estimate and without any prejudice what Candace Owens made since September 11. this is no judgement about her content and no opinion about her thoughts and assumptions regarding Charlie Kirks Assassination. He was an AmericanHero and before I know everything about the Assassination I won‘t share an opinion . 
There are a lot of stupid numbers out and without her overhead costs : 

Candace Owens: Why Sept 11–Oct 30 likely ≥ $2M — and plausibly more

Baseline math (conservative):
• ~87M YouTube views in the window.
• Use mainstream creator RPM $3–$6 (money to creator per 1k views).
→ Ads: $260k–$520k
• Live features + memberships (Super Chats/Thanks at ~70% to creator; member rev share similar):
→ $100k–$300k
• Sponsors/affiliates (PureTalk, Nimi Skincare; plus rotating podcast advertisers). Large-channel integrations commonly price $40k+ per integration or by CPM. Multiple lives + clipped segments stack several reads/week:
→ $800k–$1.4M

Baseline total: ≈ $1.8M–$2.2M
(That’s why “≈$2M” already makes sense.)

⸻

Why it could be more (upside levers most people ignore):
1.Q4 uplift & event-driven ad fill
Advertisers spend more in Sept–Dec and during news spikes. Even a +20–40% RPM bump on parts of this window moves ads closer to $450k–$700k without assuming wild rates.
2.Mid-roll density & long watch time
Talk shows and lives support extra mid-rolls. If average ad density is higher than generic uploads, effective RPM rises (more monetized impressions per view).
https://t.co/ua4cLB7Zwf Premium share
Creators also get paid from Premium watch time. A +5–15% add on top of ad RPM is common for long-form channels → nudges the ad bucket upward.
https://t.co/jcplcPKIG2 monetization spikes
Big news nights = surge Super Chats (and Super Thanks on replays). A handful of outsized lives can push live/member totals toward $250k–$500k for the period.
5.Sponsor stacking across formats
It’s not just the show open. Pre-roll reads on clips, mid-rolls in long VOD, live integrations, podcast audio reads, YouTube Shorts mentions, pinned comments, description links — all can be parallel in the same week. Even at mid-market rates, this easily supports $1.2M–$1.8M for the window.
6.Affiliate/CPA upside (telco/consumer DTC)
Telco and DTC wellness/beauty often include hybrid deals (flat fee + CPA/RevShare). If conversion spikes during a viral month, affiliate tail can add a 6-figure kicker over the base sponsorship fees.
7.Back-catalog uplift
Viral months lift older videos. Those extra millions of catalog views quietly add to ads + Premium without any extra production.
8.Off-platform revenue (if used)
Simulcasts/clips on X, Rumble, podcast platforms (dynamic ad insertion), speaking fees, merch — not required for the $2M case, but they can add meaningful upside if active.

⸻

Ceiling case (still sane, not heroic):
• Ads: $450k–$700k (Q4 uplift + Premium + density)
• Live/members: $250k–$500k (a few outsized lives)
• Sponsors/affiliates: $1.2M–$1.8M (stacked reads across lives, clips, podcast + CPA kicker)
→ Total plausible: $1.9M–$3.0M for Sept 11–Oct 30

That’s why “≈$2M” is not just plausible — it’s the midpoint of a very reasonable range once you account for (1) stacked sponsors, (2) live spikes, (3) Q4 uplift, and (4) Premium/affiliate tail. No need to pretend the RPM is $25; the mix does the heavy lifting.

cases.title:
My 47-minute workday:

Today I woke up, turned on the timer, and got straight into working on my $53k/mo YouTube Shorts channel.

I wanted to see how long it actually takes me to complete my daily tas
cases.content:
My 47-minute workday:

Today I woke up, turned on the timer, and got straight into working on my $53k/mo YouTube Shorts channel.

I wanted to see how long it actually takes me to complete my daily tasks.

46 minutes and 50 seconds.

1/ I approved the ideas for the week that my scriptwriters found 

2/ I approved 4 scripts they wrote and added them to our content CRM 

3/ I chose 2 videos out of 25 we had prepared and scheduled them 

4/ I checked the content CRMs and my team chats to make sure there were no issues and everything was running smoothly

And that was it.

I turned off the timer at 07:04 a.m.

Most people were just waking up and driving to their 9–5 that pays them $36 per hour.

In 47 minutes, I finished the work that makes me $2k/day.

And the fun thing is, there are just 4 Fundamental Systems that allow me to do that 👇

(it only takes a week to set them up, so if you're a YouTuber — save this tweet)

Number #1: Ideation Framework

For this channel, we have only three key methods we use to find viral ideas:

* Our custom GPT prompts
* Viral Format Transfer
* Competitor Research

I turned them into step-by-step checklists so scriptwriters always know where to find new ideas. 

Then my Ideation SOP makes sure I only need to look at it once a week.

Number #2: Content Guidelines

For each process in my team (e.g., scripting, animation, music, sound design, subtitles), there’s a guideline and a checklist. 

Some are 1-pagers (like the music guideline), 

others have more than 100 pages (like the animation guideline).

They help me avoid revisions and standardize the entire production process.

Number #3: Content CRM

This is a task manager for the entire team that helps me avoid micromanagement and keeps everyone on track.

I get notifications when someone isn’t doing the work they need to, so I can quickly diagnose and fix the issue.

--

Every month, I keep adding new systems that help me spend less and less time managing the channel, and more time doing the strategic stuff I actually want to do.

“Trenches” are your personal choice.

At the end of the day, you’re running a channel - not the other way around.

For the past month, I’ve been helping other Faceless YouTubers structure their workflow and run their channels as businesses.

One of them went from 70m views per month to over 210m in just 40 days. 

Another just started posting and got 28m views from his first 4 videos.

If you’d like me to personally guide you on building those SOPs, DM me “The Art Of YouTube” and let’s chat.

Total revenue
cases.content:
I just watched a creator expose his faceless YouTube channel that made $14,000 in ONE MONTH.

Here's what shocked me (and why I'm building my app faster): 🧵

The niche: Gladiator History
Total revenue shown: $14,000/month
RPM: $7.48 (that's INSANE for YouTube)

One video alone: 300K views = $2,815

And get this... he started in APRIL.

Here's the monthly breakdown:

April: $66
May: $11
July: $15
August: $5,900
Last 28 days: $9,157

That's exponential growth in less than 6 months.

From $66 to $9K/month.

With a FACELESS channel.

His process for EACH video:

1. Google Interesting Gladiator History (2-3)
2. Download as PDFs
3. Feed to ChatGPT with a prompt
4. Generate script from REAL data
5. Use ElevenLabs for voice
6. Edit visuals
7. Post

Time per video: Under 2 hours.

But here's the kicker:

He's not alone.

His students are replicating this:

- Jason: Got monetized month 1, made $1K month 2
- Brad: 100K views on his 4th upload
- Ismo: Monetized in under a month, made $1,700
- Will: 7K views in 3 days (2nd upload)

The pattern I'm seeing:

✅ Pick a micro-niche (specific state + real estate)
✅ Use AI for scripts (based on real news)
✅ Use AI for voice (ElevenLabs)
✅ Simple editing (or hire for $20)
✅ Consistency

Results: $1K-$14K/month within 3-6 months

Now here's why this matters to ME:

He's doing this manually.

2 hours per video.
Multiple tools.
Copy/paste between platforms.
Download/upload cycles.

What if you could do this in 5 minutes?

That's what I'm building.

My app will automate:

- Script Research
- Script generation (using real sources)
- AI voiceover generation
- Image/video creation
- Basic editing/compilation

Everything he does in 2 hours → 5 minutes with 1 click.

Same quality. 24x faster.

Why this works in 2025:

- YouTube LOVES consistent uploaders
- Algorithm kicks in at video 20-30
- Most people quit at video 15
- Reason? It takes too much time

If I can cut production time by 95%, people will actually stick around long enough to see results.

The math is simple:

His method: 2 hours/video
My app: 5 mins/video

If he posts 3x/week = 6 hours
With my app = 15 minutes

That's 5.75 hours saved per week.
23 hours per month.
276 hours per year.

Time = money.

He made $14K/month doing this manually.

Imagine if he could post 2x more because production is 24x faster.

$14K could become $28K.

Or start 2 channels and make $28K across both.

That's the opportunity I'm building for.

If you want a faceless YouTube channel but don't have 10+ hours/week to dedicate:

Follow my journey.

I'm building the tool that makes this possible for anyone.

$14K/month is life-changing money.

And it shouldn't require 40 hours/week to get there.

Let's build.

cases.content:
SECOND viral YouTube video for our biggest doctor brand 🔥 

100k+ views in only 5 days. This is the fastest video to go viral for this channel over the last 15 years. 

It’s still rocking at at 500 views per hour, and 93.9% of the traffic is from the algorithm itself (that free, high-quality traffic everybody’s after).

Over HALF of the viewers have never heard of the channel before, so we’re bringing NEW people into our client’s world.

Our last viral video from a few weeks ago is on 170,000 views. We sent a screenshot to this client and they were absolutely over the moon about it.

After taking an almost 2-year break from content, and even then, only getting around 2,000 views per video…

We’ve already helped four videos get 12,900 views, 13,000 views, 100,000 views, and even 170,000 views…

All in a little over our FIRST month together.

But my favorite part?

The main intangible benefit we bring to clients?

And why YouTube is undoubtedly the best platform?

Watch time.

Since working with this client, we’ve added 48,750 HOURS of added watch time. That’s 12x more than the previous period before working with us.

That’s enough to walk around Earth 25 times or watch The Titanic 14,985 in a row. Or in simpler words, it’s 5.5 years of attention.

This is what I call building a DEEP BRAND.

Every smart marketer will tell you it’s all about shifting beliefs. It’s about getting your market to BELIEVE what you need them to, so they will follow you, rave about you, and buy from you.

What better way to shift beliefs than by publishing 15m-2hr content, sharing all the details and nuance you want, teaching your market everything you know?

This is Deep Branding.

I tell all my clients that YouTube is the hardest platform.

But that’s also what makes it the best.

Onto the next one 💪

cases.title:
Yo, I'm launching a new interview series with entrepreneurs who've absolutely CRUSHED IT with YouTube💰

Going deep with creators who transformed from 
$0 → $10k, $20k, $50k/month and beyond.

Will be
cases.content:
Yo, I'm launching a new interview series with entrepreneurs who've absolutely CRUSHED IT with YouTube💰

Going deep with creators who transformed from 
$0 → $10k, $20k, $50k/month and beyond.

Will be breaking down their journey, what actually works for them, how they scaled their operations, the tools they use, and the REAL behind-the-scenes stuff nobody talks about.

First episode is dropping RIGHT NOW

Interview #1: From Homeless to $25K Per Month - The Raw Journey of @imatteo 

Today I'm interviewing someone who perfectly embodies the phrase "started from the bottom." 

Just 12 months ago, he was literally homeless with zero income, yet managed to build a faceless YouTube empire that now generates up to $25K monthly.

Background and Beginnings 

What attracted you to the faceless YouTube channel model, and what motivated you to start building channels without appearing on camera? 

I've always liked YouTube, cinema, making videos. From a very young age, I was already recording video clips for my friends and learning about cameras and such things. But I've never liked being the center of attention. It overwhelms me quite a bit. That's how I "discovered" faceless channels, and in a way, I had been consuming content creators who didn't show their faces for years, but I unconsciously associated it with the traditional YouTuber figure. It wasn't until I saw an interview with Noah that I clicked and said, wait a moment, I can do this, and that's where it all started. But my main motivation was to get out of bad situations I had in my life at that moment. I was homeless, I had nowhere to stay, no family, no friends, no money, nothing. YouTube was my last "option" because I got carried away by the shiny object syndrome and tried dropshipping, themed TikTok pages, etc., without analyzing what I was good at and what I could succeed at in some way with the skills and resources I had at that time. YouTube was what best suited me.

How long have you been managing faceless channels, and what niches or content genres have you explored so far?

I've been doing this for exactly 1 year, but I didn't start making money from YouTube until well into August 2024 when I received my first check for $500. Before that, it was a period of learning because I literally had to try and test things. I didn't have a job or any source of income to pay for a course or coach, so I had to dedicate an insane amount of time to learning. No one on Twitter answered my questions, so I was completely adrift and without guidance. Still, I decided to learn, consuming everything I could on Twitter/YouTube, etc. But yes, technically I've been on YouTube for a year, but it wasn't until August that I made my first money; before that, it was all $0. For niches, I like to create content more focused on older audiences. All my life I was surrounded by elderly people, and I didn't have any specialized knowledge to share on YouTube like finance or those things, so I went with what I knew best.

As more people join automated faceless YouTube channels, have you noticed changes in the landscape? How have you adapted as competition increases?

In the last 3 months, I have noticed a significant increase in competitors, and the most difficult thing for me to compete with was that they specialized tremendously in sub-niches. At the beginning of my journey, what I noticed was that there were only 2 or 3 moderately large channels, but they were specialized. I went for something more general and tried to encompass different topics, and that's how I succeeded. But once you get many very viral videos on different topics, it's difficult to maintain those types of audiences. Some subscribe for x video, others for another video, and when you release a video that doesn't interest audience 1, YouTube, so to speak, penalizes you a bit. To adapt to the competition, I've had to re-specialize in the sub-niches that have worked best for me, but without much success.

How long did it take for your first channel to become profitable, and was there a key moment when it really took off? 

My first channel took 4 months to become profitable. And it took off in October 2024 (Q4) when it started generating $22k-$25k per month for about 3 or 4 consecutive months. Then entering Q1, the story was different, although it still remains a very stable source of income given the number of subscribers.

Did you have mentors or role models when you started, or any particular channel that inspired your approach?

I didn't have mentors as such. But I did join 2 other creators who were "starting" at the same time as me. They were quite more advanced than me, but we decided to join and create a kind of group where we shared lessons, feedback, support, ideas among all of us. That's how all 3 of us managed to take off quite a bit. Each one made $20K per month from those of us who came out of that group.

Content Creation and Operations 

What does a typical day look like for you in managing your YouTube channels? 

My days are very chaotic; I don't have an organization as such. I suffer from constant distractions, but basically, my entire day is waking up, exercising, going shopping, and then sitting at the computer to research, look for ideas, talk with the team, review strategies, create scripts, think about new niches, etc. I do many things I shouldn't do so many, but that's how it is.

How do you generate ideas for content or choose niches for a new channel? 

It depends a lot on the type of content. I don't have a fixed strategy for looking for ideas or choosing niches. Basically, I'm on YouTube all day. Watching videos, looking for ideas on different accounts. Sometimes I see a title format that works extremely well, and I write it down, go to ChatGPT, and try to see how that title could work in other niches, then I go and check if that format exists. Another way is to see a winning title format again and try to see how much more that same title format can be exploited within that same niche or in other niches. Where I always look first is in the titles of the videos. I feel that if a title format works, there's some emotional and psychological trigger that makes that title work in any other niche. Then also the thumbnails, obviously. For niches, what I do is combine a bit of a niche that has a lot of demand with a format that hasn't been done in that niche that has proven success that it works, and I try to see how it goes.

Can you explain your content creation process from idea to uploaded video (for example, script, voiceover, editing, and publishing)?

The idea often arises from combining titles with different hooks. Sometimes I look in channels in my niche for old videos with great potential that haven't been fully exploited, or channels that my audience is also interested in, and try to do the same. Once I have a vague idea of what I want to do, I go to ChatGPT and try to create as many possible combinations of the title, giving it different angles, etc. When I've decided what to do, I make the script following my style and template, and I rely on different AI tools to create them. Voiceover with Eleven Labs, we put that in Trello, and we start producing. The editor is in charge of creating the edit following the guidelines we gave him. The publication is in charge of our VAs. They have the task of putting the description and optimizing all the upload elements of the video.

What tools or software have become essential in your workflow?

Many. But mainly ChatGPT and Claude. I would say that those two tools are 70%-80% of everything I do. I give them many uses. Also the typical ones, Discord, Trello, Canva, etc. I've even used Twitter to look for ideas or support myself with something.

Do you work with a team or outsource any part of the content production? If so, how did you find and manage your team?

Yes, I work with a team. This has been one of my weak points throughout the process because it's always been very difficult for me to communicate well, hire quickly, and make my collaborators understand what I want to do. Currently, I have a core team of about 4 people, and we support ourselves with other collaborators who come and go.

Growth and Monetization 

What are your main monetization methods? Have you stayed primarily with AdSense, or have you diversified with sponsorships, merchandise, or other income sources? 

AdSense only so far.

Many refer to automated channels as "cash cow" channels for their focus on generating income. Do you ever feel tension between pursuing views and maintaining quality? How do you balance profitability with offering value to the audience? 

Yes. In fact, my approach at the beginning was quality over quantity, then I felt somewhat pressured to create quantity over quality, and it didn't work very well for me, and I didn't know how to adapt to that approach. Still, I think each type of content has its approach. There are niches where quantity is most important, others where a bit more quality instead of quantity is what will work for you. It depends a lot on the niche in my opinion, although I think there's room for everything. The most difficult thing is to continue producing fresh ideas while you do quantity if you don't have a strict format to follow.

Challenges and Lessons 

What have been some of the biggest challenges or setbacks you've faced in managing faceless channels?

The biggest challenge for me has undoubtedly been overthinking things a lot. Fear of failure, and lack of team management. As I said before, the biggest challenge for me has been that, making a decision to move forward and move quickly, and it's not for lack of ability since I've launched random channels that I've abandoned, and the first video has gotten more than 100K views in 1 week. It's a matter of organization, of having systems. That has been my Achilles heel, but I am committed to changing that quickly.

Did you ever reach the point of considering abandoning or drastically changing your approach? What happened, and what made you decide to continue? 

Abandon YouTube? Never. I couldn't. This is my life. I plan to be here for the next 20-30 years. Even if YouTube ceases to exist, I will be on the next platform that exists. I couldn't do anything else now. I'm not here to make quick money (although it's welcome). But no, abandoning YouTube or my path has NEVER been an option to consider. Yes, sometimes you want to do better and want to have better results and get angry with yourself, but then you think that this is for life. I'll be here until I die, so it's normal if in my first year I'm not making $100K a month. Although it's not an excuse to pat myself on the back, quite the opposite.

How do you handle pressure with changes on the YouTube platform (algorithm updates, policy changes, etc.)? Have you had to adjust your approach due to these changes? 

Not really. Although I've been on YouTube for a short time as a creator, I've been consuming YouTube all my life. I've seen the changes without being part of the industry. You learn to adapt. Play by the rules and try to be there on the edge of what's possible. Although I've never had serious problems. And if I did, well, learn from them and move on. It's adapt or die. I prefer to adapt.

In your opinion, what is the worst part of managing a faceless YouTube operation and why? 

The worst part of managing an operation is having a team that is not committed. That makes you fall behind more, that you have to do things yourself because your team doesn't do them. Having to chase after them to do their job. That's the most complicated part of the operation. Not having A players on your team to make your life simpler. Maybe I don't know how to hire well.

Reflections and Advice 

What drives you to continue creating content and developing channels, even on difficult days? 

That's a good question. My resilience. Never in my life have I been one to give up. I may fall a thousand times, but I will keep getting up. It doesn't matter. I'm not the smartest guy in the class. In fact, I find it very difficult to understand simple things; I'm quite dumb. But I'm always there in the trenches, no matter what. When I made $0 a month, when I made $25K a month, and when I make $100K a month or if I go back to $0 a month. I have no motivation. I just get up and do it. It's just that I wouldn't find myself doing anything else in my life beyond making YouTube videos. That's my engine. This is what I do, and that's it. If things go badly, well, I try to figure out why, but it has never crossed my mind to want to abandon it... I wouldn't know what to do afterward in my life. The day after that, I think I would die.

Would you say you've already "made it" as a YouTube entrepreneur? If not, what would "making it" look like for you? 

No. I still don't feel like I've "made it." I don't even know if I'll ever feel that. I've felt moments of "look how far we've come," but to say I've already made it, this is the goal... I think I'll never experience that because just as I have no plan to ever abandon it, there is no goal. My path here started to last as long as I last on this planet. In one way or another, my whole life will be linked to this.

In your opinion, what is the most important quality or skill for a YouTube creator to be successful, especially managing a faceless channel? 

Resilience. It's not a business of making money fast. Nor is it something that always stays high. The most important quality is knowing how to get up when you fall. No matter what happens. Get up and keep walking. That's the most important thing. You can know absolutely nothing, but if you dedicate enough time to learn and move forward, at some point you will arrive. No matter how.

Why do you think you've been able to succeed in an area where many others struggle or abandon? 

By not abandoning and being completely obsessed with achieving it and having a clear goal. Now I don't have a clear goal; at the time, I did. It wasn't money; it was getting out of misery, being able to pay myself rent, and having enough money to go shopping. That was all. Now the goals have been diluted. But I keep going no matter what; it's not like I'm on an island sunbathing. I don't take vacations.

What's the best advice you've received about content creation or online business? 

I've received many good pieces of advice from people I respect and admire very much, and I'm always attentive and receptive to any advice or help, but if I had to choose just 1 piece of advice, it would be "be obsessed" from Alex Hormozi. It's not precisely about YouTube, but it's advice I always carry with me when I feel a little lost.

What have you learned in the last 6 months that has helped you better manage your channels? 

Many things. Especially in the part of strategy/finding ideas/looking at YouTube with a more analytical and business eye to detect patterns.

What is a mistake that you still struggle to avoid in managing your channels? Any advice on how to avoid it? 

Moving quickly and building teams. It's a mistake that I still struggle with since I always have too many things in my head. Putting the focus on one thing and steering the ship in a well-marked direction. The shiny object syndrome is what most makes me lose focus on what really matters and comparing myself to others. The advice I would give is to look at where you were a year ago. Look at where you are now. Everyone has their context, their background, their skills, personal situations, etc. Don't focus on what others achieve or do, but focus on what you need to improve right now to be where you want to get to. In the end, nobody is going to be with you in a difficult moment to extend a hand to you except a few, so care little about what others do; you try to do the best you can every day. If you fall, get up again and always try to be the best person possible no matter who you have in front of you. Always do good and don't look at what others do.

If you could give a single piece of advice to someone starting a faceless YouTube channel tomorrow, what would it be and why? 

Don't despair and try to learn first. Many jump into the pool without mentally preparing themselves or even learning what they are doing. That proactivity is good, but statistics say you're going to fail, so if you fail, you don't even know why you're failing, and you're going to get depressed. So first learn, prepare mentally, and most importantly, have a why. A clear goal. Why are you doing it? What is your motivation to do it? If you don't know, you won't achieve it. Only those who have a clear, well-defined goal are those who are capable of going beyond and going the extra mile.

What was your most important day or unforgettable moment on YouTube, one that you will never forget no matter what? 

When I monetized my first channel. That feeling won't have any comparison. I think that's the closest moment to an "I made it" that I've had in my life. Because it gave me hope that I was finally going to generate money and could get out of my situation. It was one of the happiest moments of my life; it may seem stupid, but that's how it was.

Goals and Future Plans 

What goals do you set for your YouTube business, and how do you define success for the next phase of your journey? 

My goal mainly in this phase is to learn from scratch again. To be more proactive and have a clear execution strategy. To get back on track. Hire and surround myself with good people, A players with a good vision of things. Trustworthy people. And in the long term, continue scaling until having enough money to make larger and more ambitious productions.

What are your future plans or ambitions for your YouTube career? Do you foresee launching new channels, experimenting with different formats, or even appearing on camera someday? 

Maybe in the future, I do plan to appear in front of the camera, but with a more serious format, like a podcast/TV show, those things. With a set. And not talking about business or those things. Simply talking about things I like, a more television format, me being the presenter. Something like that. That would be my future pinnacle. That's where I see my career could go without leaving aside, of course, my faceless channels or that side of the business.

Complete the Phrases 

Most YouTube creators would be better off if they... think long term. 

What separates the best creators from the rest is... focus, planning, and resilience. 

A good YouTube creator should never... 
release videos just because they have to follow a calendar. 

The biggest mistake about managing a YouTube channel is... 
hiring the wrong people.

⚡️ Quick Round 

This or That 

Viral videos or evergreen videos? 
Evergreen. 

Solo work or team collaboration? 
Team if you want to grow is crucial. 

Speed or perfection? 
Speed. 

AI scripts or human-written? 
AI. 

Thumbnail first or title first? 
Title first. 

Scale in breadth (more channels) or scale in depth (better content)? 
Both. 

YouTube automation or personal branding? 
Both.

cases.content:
Which one of my client's channels do you think makes more money? 

Left or Right? 

I will give you a hint... (it's not the left one). 

But here's the thing... 

Without the channel on the left, the right wouldn't make as much money. 

Amplify Views is broad, targeting anyone who wants to grow their YouTube channel...

And Linden Chasteen is targeting specifically business owners. 

How does a video with a few hundred views generate thousands of dollars? 

It’s all about getting the right people to watch. 

Amplify Views = social proof + large volume 

Linden Chasteen = turns viewers into buyers

This is the biggest mistake most business owners make… 

They focus only on views instead of who is actually watching.

If you are trying to close clients from your personal brand, views alone are not an important metric. 

The strategy behind these two channels works together to maximize growth and revenue. 

That's how I was able to help my client generate $50,000 per month organically.

cases.title:
Scaling a faceless YouTube channel to $100K/month updates

A year ago this channel was pulling in $20K/month

Not life changing money but decent

Then I basically said “Eh, not worth my time” and pivo
cases.content:
Scaling a faceless YouTube channel to $100K/month updates

A year ago this channel was pulling in $20K/month

Not life changing money but decent

Then I basically said “Eh, not worth my time” and pivoted to FCA

FCA blew up. Multi 6-figure months

..but the channel?

Fell apart

Like bad

Dropped to $3K/month. Just barely covering expenses

And now that FCA is cruising so I decided… screw it

I’m going back into the channels

But this time we’re doing it right

No more churn and burn. No more throwing spaghetti at the wall

I want this channel doing $100K/month on autopilot. A real brand. A sellable asset

And let me tell you... It’s been BRUTAL

--

What It Actually Takes to Build a $100K/Month Faceless Channel:

The first step we realized we were in a niche with a ceiling. So we pivoted to a bigger one

Which meant… we had to rebuild the entire team.

Channel manager? Brought someone new on that was excited and full of energy and optimistic for results

Scriptwriter? spent a lot of time directing him (honestly might need to replace)

Editors? Had to cycle through a few.

Thumbnail designers? 3-4 before we found one who gets it

Voiceover? Energy is good but audio sucks

Then we produced 20 videos

And took 10 of them down

Deleted every video under 10K views.

Why? Because 90% of failures were weak hooks and packaging

I don't want a bunch of dead flowers next to a bunch of pretty ones. It makes the channel look ugly and not trustworthy

So we spent another 4 hours on a Zoom call JUST coming up with video ideas and a SOP

Now? We’re only making 2 video formats - the ones that actually work.

First video is what you see below in the screenshot of refocusing our ideation again after 3 months

Then we realized that all the videos were flops because of hooks so we spent another 4 hours on a Zoom call JUST writing hooks

3-6 hooks per video. Rank them. Pick the best one.

--

Here’s what blows my mind about building a branded faceless channel:

The depth of effort behind every single video is insane

Like… 1,000 hours per video insane

But this is what you have to do if you want videos that pull a million+ views on repeat

Once we lock that in..

$100K/month off ONE channel

And not just from YouTube ad revenue

Brand deals (because it’s an actual brand)

Longevity (no overnight algorithm deaths)

A sellable asset (this thing will print money for YEARS)

I used to churn and burn faceless channels like all the gurus you see online.

Got to $70K/month doing it. But it was a grind

Constantly cycling through niches, trends, teams, stress through the roof

Not this time

This time we’re building something that lasts

Follow for more updates I'll be posting along the way

cases.title:
big problem YouTube businessmen face is scaling

not hard to start a channel and make $5k–10k per mo

even on your own or by managing a micro team of freelancers

but how to hit high 5 or 6 figures pe
cases.content:
big problem YouTube businessmen face is scaling

not hard to start a channel and make $5k–10k per mo

even on your own or by managing a micro team of freelancers

but how to hit high 5 or 6 figures per month without  years of stress,

like my student @JaroslavYT ?

There are 3 main pillars of that:

1. Mindset

This is super easy to keep in mind, but hard to show in action

Too often, people are afraid of big numbers, simply not ready for them

And they want to do a “lifestyle business,” or be artists

Artists value each video as a masterpiece and forget that, mostly, social media is a game of volume

Businessmen test formats, niches, team members, etc. so fast with systems, because it is a real business

2. Niches

It’s obvious if you choose low-volume and low-RPM niches, 

it will be harder to ever hit 6 figures per month 

(not even mentioning niche competition or production complexity)

Although I don’t like the term “YouTube Automation,” it makes sense if it’s tackled properly

Only during 2024 did we have many high-volume niches where it was easy to hit 6 figures, such as:

- Politics,
- Celebrity Drama,
- AI channels,
- Sports news, etc

And it happens every year

I’m 100% sure we will see new rises in 2025

3. Team systems

This is the hardest but the most important step because

it requires not only hard skills like management and analysis, 

but also soft skills - an understanding of human psychology

But to be honest, it’s super doable; 

for most people, the biggest cornerstone is that 

they don’t even want to scale to big numbers because 

it requires some “boring” work, like creating SOPs.

I’m just sharing what’s on my mind, I know these advanced tweets won’t get as much exposure 

and won’t even be interesting for dopamine blasting 

like tweets saying “how to hit $10k per month with YT” but I don’t care.

If you want me to record a video about the team structure and systems I’ve implemented, 

leave a comment, if there are many requests, I’ll do that

cases.title:
4 faceless YouTube channels = $160k per month.

Here’s how you can work toward this level—it’s tough, but very achievable with the right moves:

1. Build a network of “killers.” Surround yourself with
cases.content:
4 faceless YouTube channels = $160k per month.

Here’s how you can work toward this level—it’s tough, but very achievable with the right moves:

1. Build a network of “killers.” Surround yourself with serious faceless YouTube creators. Seek out communities, follow key Twitter pages, and make connections with people in the space. Hiring skilled people from my network has been instrumental to scaling my channels.

2. Prioritize quality over quantity. Early on, focus on high-quality videos rather than trying to keep up with channels posting 2-5 times daily. Mastering what makes a video go viral should come first; then, gradually increase your frequency as you build consistency.

3. Analyze what’s working in your niche. Regularly study top-performing videos in your niche. Look for patterns in their structure, titles, and thumbnails, and identify the hooks that keep viewers engaged. Building a channel requires a solid understanding of what works for your audience.

4. Invest in an efficient content pipeline. Once quality is up, create a streamlined production process with dedicated roles for scriptwriting, voiceover, editing, and uploading. A smooth workflow allows for scalability without burning out or compromising video standards.

5. Master the data game. Use analytics to inform every step: optimize thumbnails, titles, and even posting times based on your metrics. Channels that succeed long-term continuously use data to refine their strategies.

If you enjoyed this post:
1. Follow me @FacelessChems for more
2. RT to share it with others

cases.title:
🤑This is how many views it takes to make $1,000,000 a year making YouTube videos:

YouTube Shorts:

With a $0.25 rpm (standard english nieche rpm now, even higher sometimes) you will need 333,000,000
cases.content:
🤑This is how many views it takes to make $1,000,000 a year making YouTube videos:

YouTube Shorts:

With a $0.25 rpm (standard english nieche rpm now, even higher sometimes) you will need 333,000,000 monthly views for 12 months straight to make atleast $1,000,000 by the end of the year.

Yes, It sure is a lot of views, but I have achieved multiple months with over 333M views doing shorts. Its not hard if you have a good nieche and are posting 2-5x a day and staying consistent. You can hire tons of editors and directors on fiverr, discord, onlinejobs, instagram, X, etc, to help you out with this. The standard pay for editors is $10-$80 a short, Just depends what nieche you are doing, and how big your channel is. Rememeber to make every video high quality and try to get as many subs as you can off the video as well. This will help u stabilize consistent views, making it easier to reach this goal of 333M a month.

Long form:

With a $5 RPM (Easy to get if you are in an english nieche, and posting videos over 10 minutes and placing ads every 50-75 seconds), you only need 560,000 views a day or 16,800,000 views a month to become a millionare by the end of the year.

There are COUNTLESS channels that are reaching this goal, probably over 25,000+ if i'd have to say. And I cant stress it enough when people say its hard to reach this goal and they cant do it. Absolutely anyone can reach this goal, it is easier than you would ever expect if you just put your 100% effort into it. Make sure ur spending a lot of time finding a good monitizable nieche that you know u can maintain and have fun doing. Again, hire editors, managers, directors, thumbnail artists, and even invest into a pre-monitized channel if that helps you. Ive bought tons of them over the past couple months and it works wonders. I have recently got into long form myself, and I was always scared to start, but never realised how easy it truly was, it just takes a lot more time per video than a short does, which is something that makes a lot of people give up. If thats your case, just stick to doing shorts. But I promise its worth a shot starting a long form channel, even if its on the side :)

Im literally 16 years old and am on my path to reaching BOTH long form and short form views/month to become a millionare within a year. You guys can do anything just push through, and put the work in.👊

PS: ⚠ the screenshot below is not real, I sure wish it was xD, I used inspect element, just used it as an example

cases.content:
Some of my favorite faceless YouTube niches at the moment..

Travel:
Though it takes time to gain momentum, this niche is a treasure trove of consistent income. Once it gains traction, its evergreen nature ensures a steady stream of revenue, making it a reliable long-term investment.

Basketball:

LWhile competitive, basketball is one of YouTube’s largest niches. The initial challenge of copyright issues can be mitigated by partnering with the NBA for revenue sharing. The NBA’s progressive stance on social media makes this collaborating with them to get the rights on their content pretty lucrative. Plus, with numerous sports channels as inspiration, it’s a great niche for beginners to get into faceless YouTube.

Anime:
This niche offers a massive audience and significant revenue potential. The challenge lies in navigating copyright issues, as some studios are stricter than others. However, once you crack the code, the rewards is substantial.

Health:
YouTube’s evolving health policies require savvy navigation, but once mastered, this niche offers tremendous upside. Despite the initial hurdles, the potential for significant earnings is enormous, making it worth the effort. Seen a lot of beginners get into it over the past year and it’s been solid.

There’s many trends that come and go but for niches that you can get to $10k per month in decent timeframe and KNOW it’ll be around for a while, these are my favorite faceless YouTube channel niches!

You’re welcome 👊

cases.title:
By all metrics - Charlie Morgan is a small YouTuber.

He only posts 4 videos per month, and these only average around 35,000 views 📊

You’d be forgiven for not knowing him. However;

He has made a who
cases.content:
By all metrics - Charlie Morgan is a small YouTuber.

He only posts 4 videos per month, and these only average around 35,000 views 📊

You’d be forgiven for not knowing him. However;

He has made a whopping $18.5M to date 💰

- Charlie Morgans EXACT YouTube Strategy 📊
- How To Dominate ANY Niche 🚀
- EXACTLY How you can do the same 💵
- What videos you SHOULD be making 🎥

The Pivot 📈

To understand how we got to this point, we have to take a step back towards the end of last year.

Charlie had been posting the same type of content consistently for 2-years. 

His videos looked the same as everyone else's.

It’s fair to say, the niche was very saturated - making it very difficult for Charlie to stand out.

He very rarely even broke past 10,000 views.

To win on YouTube, you need to consistently stand out. 

💡And there’s one way to do that.

It’s to create content that nobody else can.

His Moat 🌊

MrBeast invests an insane amount of money into each video.

Far more than any other YouTuber can afford to. For this reason, he’s able to consistently create the most impressive spectacles on YouTube.

However, when starting out, he didn’t have any money.

But he did have time.

I kid you not this is a real MrBeast video;

Before he had anything material to invest into his content; he invested his time.

Simply because nobody else was willing to put in as much time - MrBeast won. 

He was creating content that, at the time, nobody else wanted to make.

That irreplacable factor that makes your video stand out is called your moat 🌊

The same way a castle’s moat stops invaders from attacking - a content moat stops competitors from copying you 🏰

You need to define your moat - and Charlie Morgan did just that - but rather accidentally…

The Video 🚀

On December 25th, he decided to try something new.

While his competitors were making the same scripted, high production-quality talking-head videos. 

What Charlie posted was different.

It was a whiteboard video, with a very simple thumbnail - titled;

“How to Become a Millionaire When You’re Broke”.

Over the following weeks - this video would climb to nearly 300,000 views 📊

While Charlie’s video might not seem impressive at first, very few people can talk coherently and provide valuable information for an hour. 

At the time, few creators were making this type of content - 

but clearly there was an audience hungry for it.

Since the video was posted - Charlie has used this same moat repeatedly - and had great results 🚀

Currently - he averages around 35,000 views per video.

So now that we understand how Charlie Morgan is able to get the attention of this audience - but how does he make over $1M per month from this?

It's simple.

1) You’re a bored viewer - so you go on YouTube. Charlie’s video came up.

2) You resonated with the title. The goal of becoming a millionaire has been in your mind for a while.

3) You’ve tried everything - but nothing has worked out.

4) So you decide to give it a click.

5) You enjoy the video. You find it valuable, as well as entertaining. You get a sense of accomplishment and motivation after watching.

6) You may even watch another video by him afterwards.

7) Slowly but surely, you build trust in Charlie Morgan. You start to view him as an authority.

8) You start to see him as a means to a change. To finally achieve the goal you thought was lost.

9) In your mind - Charlie’s name, face, and information becomes associated with achieving your goals. 

10) You slowly convince yourself that Charlie Morgan is the only way to achieve your goal.

11) And so you make the decision to do something about it.

In the description of every Charlie Morgan video, he has a link. 

It takes you to a landing page - where he promises you that he can help you clients.

Charlie Morgan runs a business called The Imperium Agency. A client acquisition agency. And this is how Charlie Morgan makes $1m/month.

The Strategy 📊

7 hours, 11 touch points across 4 locations.

This is the outcome of a study conducted by Google aiming to find out what it takes to turn someone into a customer.

On average, a person needs to consume 7 hours of your content, have 11 touch-points with you, across 4 different locations - before they purchase from you.

7 hours of content consumed is almost impossible to achieve with short-form - or on any other social-media platform.

Even with daily newsletters, it can take a lot more time to build up that authority than long-form youtube content once per month.

Charlie’s videos tend to range from 30-minutes in length up to past the hour mark.

He VERY quickly builds up the 7-hours of exposure with his viewers. How about the 11 touch-points? He posts once per week - and has a large library of previous content which viewers can binge. 

If a viewer enjoyed one of his videos - YouTube will easily promote them to several more over the span of the next few weeks.

Charlie’s channel is a marketing machine. It is the perfect mechanism for him to not only get attention, but to nurture that attention into customers ready to throw their money at him.

But what Charlie does is not unique. Many channels in the business space do the exact same thing.

And it’s becoming more and more prevalent.

So, how can you repeat it for yourself?

The Final Piece - How can you do it 👱

Here’s a very simple 5-step plan so you can copy Charlie’s exact content strategy - by using up no more than 20-minutes of time per day.

1) Identify your target audience.

Who do you want to speak to? This is by far the most important - yet undervalued step with YouTube growth. Far too many creators skip this step - or do it half-heartedly.

Dedicate a serious amount of time to figuring out the specifics of who you want to target. My advice for this step is to think small. It’s easy to try to go after the biggest possible audience you can reach - but chances are - you won’t be able to compete from the beginning.

The same way Charlie Morgan targeted a subsection of the business audience who preferred raw mind-set content - find yours.

2) Identify your moat.

What makes you unique? What can you do that nobody else can? What subject can you talk about in more depth than anyone else?

3) Video ideas

Create video ideas around your moat. Your video idea is the implementation of your moat. They should all be focussed solely around what makes you unique. The title should be something that no other creator can copy. Videos that only you can make.

4) Execution 

Doesn’t have to be fancy. Make sure it aligns with your target-audience, and the formats they typically prefer to watch. Charlie Morgan . 

You don’t  need to script your videos out in full if you’re competent with speaking impromptu.

5) Packaging

The goal with your title and thumbnail is to attract your entire target-audience as best as possible - and only your target-audience. If any other viewers click on your video from outside of your target-audience - they will not enjoy it. 

This is a negative signal to YouTube 📉

6) Consistency 

Dedicate time every day to coming up with video ideas.

At the same time - consider outsourcing stages of production such as script-outlines and editing.

That's it.

Thanks for reading.

cases.title:
💸 How YouTubers Make Money with ExpertOption / EO Broker Referral Program

YouTube is no longer just about views — it’s a money engine if you know how to monetize it correctly.

One of the fastest way
cases.content:
💸 How YouTubers Make Money with ExpertOption / EO Broker Referral Program

YouTube is no longer just about views — it’s a money engine if you know how to monetize it correctly.

One of the fastest ways to earn right now is through the referral program of ExpertOption / EO Broker.

Let’s break it down step by step.

⸻

🚀 1. Understand the Earning Model

Unlike AdSense, you don’t get paid for views — you get paid for user actions.

How it works:

You share your referral link
People sign up and start trading
You earn a % from their activity

👉 Typical models:

2–4% from trading turnover
CPA (fixed payment per deposit)
Lifetime commissions

💡 One active user can generate income for months or even years.

⸻

🎯 2. Choose the Right Content Format

Most beginners fail because they create boring “platform reviews”.

What actually works is:
emotion + money + action

High-converting formats:

💥 “I made $500 in 1 hour (real or fake?)”
📉 “I lost everything — here’s why”
📊 Live trading sessions
🧠 Simple strategies for beginners
🎮 “Trade with me” (community format)

👉 The best performers:

challenges
real-time trading
success/failure stories

⸻

🔥 3. Build a Funnel (This Is Critical)

Just dropping a link in the description doesn’t work.

You need a simple funnel:

YouTube video
👉 Telegram / community
👉 trust + explanation
👉 referral link

💡 People don’t convert instantly — they need engagement.

⸻

💬 4. Use Private Trading Rooms (Game Changer)

ExpertOption offers private trading rooms.

This allows you to:

build your own trading community
let followers trade together
earn % from their activity

👉 You become:

a community leader
a guide
and a monetization hub

⸻

📈 5. Target High-Growth Regions

Best markets right now:

🇸🇦 Saudi Arabia
🇮🇳 India
🇵🇰 Pakistan

👉 Why:

lower competition
cheaper traffic
high interest in trading

💡 Pro tip:

record in English
add Arabic / Hindi subtitles
follow local trends

⸻

🎥 6. Viral Video Formula

Use this structure:

0–3 sec (HOOK)
👉 “I made $300 in 10 minutes — watch this”

3–15 sec (Intrigue)
👉 show setup

15–40 sec (Action)
👉 trades + emotions

40–60 sec (Result)
👉 profit or loss

CTA:
👉 “Link in bio / join my room”

⸻

💡 7. How to Scale Your Income

To grow faster:

post consistently (series > single videos)
show real emotions
build a Telegram community
run challenges
use social proof

⸻

⚠️ 8. Important: Trust & Positioning

Never promise guaranteed profits.

Better positioning:

trading = skill + risk
learning process
real experience

👉 Show opportunities, not fake guarantees.

⸻

💰 Final Thoughts

YouTube + ExpertOption referral program =

scalable income
recurring revenue
higher potential than AdSense

💸 One channel can generate:
👉 $1,000 – $50,000+ per month (depending on traffic & funnel)

⸻

🔥 Quick Start Plan

Pick a niche (trading / challenges / education)
Record 10 short videos
Add Telegram + referral link
Create a private trading room
Scale content

⸻
#ExpertOption #Trading #OnlineEarning #AffiliateMarketing #MakeMoneyOnline #CryptoTrading #Forex #PassiveIncome #SideHustle #DigitalMarketing #YouTubeGrowth #TradingStrategy #EarnMoney #SaudiArabia #India #Pakistan #EOBroker #TradingCommunity #FinancialFreedom #WorkOnline #IncomeOnline 🚀

⸻
