🚀 Turn on the YouTube Money Machine! (Make Dollars with AI)

With AI tools like ChatGPT and Grok, you can build a profitable YouTube channel, without showing your face or doing hard manual work.

Just
cases.content:
🚀 Turn on the YouTube Money Machine! (Make Dollars with AI)

With AI tools like ChatGPT and Grok, you can build a profitable YouTube channel, without showing your face or doing hard manual work.

Just by asking the right questions (prompting), you can generate viral ideas, engaging scripts, automate faceless videos, master SEO, and even repurpose your content.

💡 The best part? If you use Grok, thanks to its access to real-time data, you’ll get fresher and more up-to-date ideas.

📰 A lot of people want to become YouTubers but struggle because they don’t have ideas, feel uncomfortable on camera, or don’t have time to write scripts. The good news? With next-gen AI like Grok or ChatGPT, there are no more excuses. You just need to learn how to “ask” the right way (prompt engineering).

🔥 Let’s go over 5 powerful prompts that can take your YouTube channel from zero to income:

1️⃣ Find Viral, Money-Making Ideas 💡
Prompt:

"I want to create a YouTube channel about [insert niche]. Generate a list of 10 video ideas that could go viral, based on current trends and what’s working best in my niche."

👉 Replace niche with your topic (like Tech or Cooking). This prompt scans trends and gives you 10 high-potential viral ideas.

2️⃣ Scriptwriting with a Killer Hook 🎬
Prompt:

"Write a YouTube video script for [insert topic]. Include a strong hook to grab attention in the first 15 seconds, an interesting story, and a persuasive call to action."

👉 This creates a complete script using proven psychological techniques to hook viewers.

3️⃣ Faceless Channel Automation 🤖
Prompt:

"Show me how to start a faceless YouTube channel in [niche]. Use AI for scripts, AI voices for narration, and stock footage to automate everything for passive income."

👉 Perfect if you don’t want to be on camera. This shows you how to build a fully automated content machine.

4️⃣ SEO Explosion for Maximum Reach 📈
Prompt:

"Review the latest YouTube SEO trends and create a high-ranking title, description, and tags for my video on [topic]. Optimize it for maximum reach."

👉 This helps your video rank at the top when people search.

5️⃣ Turn 1 Video into 10 Pieces of Content ♻️
Prompt:

"I have a YouTube video about [topic]. Show me how to turn this into a blog post, Instagram Reel, TikTok, and Threads to increase my reach."

👉 Repurpose one piece of content across multiple platforms and grow your brand faster.

⚡ Pro Tip:
If you use Grok, since it has access to real-time data from X (Twitter), the ideas it gives you are often more trendy and up-to-date than other AI tools.

💰 Start smart, stay consistent, and let AI do the heavy lifting, your YouTube income engine is just one prompt away.

#MakeMoneyOnline #AIBusiness #ViralContent #AICreator #YoutubeSeo #YouTubeAutomation

cases.content:
Once i changed how i produced yt videos, i went from $0 stuck to $6,000 from faceless youtube in 30 days
Here's what changed:
Before: 
•Focusing on every detail  
•40+ min editing per video 
•Trying to make perfect content 
•Posting when i had idea
Then I made $0
After: 
•Stopped optimizing for perfection  
•30-40 min editing workflow (same every time) 
•Focused on watch time, not production quality 
•5-6 videos per week consistent
I started making $6,000/month ($139/day)
You don’t need best title or best editing skills
You must do this at least 100 days without changing your editing style
The reason you are stuck, your workflow is unpredictable:
It is impossible to predict how many videos are you posting next week
Where are you getting ideas
Better systems = more money
Same camera, same tools (CapCut, Claude, ElevenLabs)
Just a repeatable system

Comment this:
DM me “youtube” to scale your faceless youtube channel from $0 to $5k+ in 90 days

cases.title:
i'm going to give you the complete roadmap to making your first $ online

no fluff. no "mindset" talk. just the steps in order.

this is exactly what i'd do if i lost everything and had to start from
cases.content:
i'm going to give you the complete roadmap to making your first $ online

no fluff. no "mindset" talk. just the steps in order.

this is exactly what i'd do if i lost everything and had to start from $0 today.

DAY 1: CHOOSE YOUR WEAPON

faceless YouTube Shorts + CPA affiliate marketing.

why? $0 to start. no skills needed. no audience needed. no face needed.

sign up for Glitchy (free, $10 bonus for signing up). pick a CPA offer in a niche that gets views — finance, scary facts, motivation, health, AI.

DAY 2–3: BUILD THE CHANNELS

set up 1 YouTube channel and 1 TikTok account in your niche.

warm up the YouTube channel PROPERLY. use proxies. don't skip this step. i've seen channels with 4–5 billion cumulative views and the #1 difference between channels that blow up and channels that die is warm-up.

youtube flags fresh accounts posting immediately. warm up = 3–5 days of normal activity before you post.

DAY 4–7: CREATE AND POST

make your first 10 faceless videos.

AI voiceover (free tools everywhere). CapCut for editing (free). templates save you time.

each video: 15–20 minutes to make. post on both YouTube Shorts AND TikTok.

put your Glitchy affiliate link in every description. every. single. one.

you now have 3 income streams active: YouTube ad revenue + affiliate commissions + clipping revenue when you redistribute.

WEEK 2: VOLUME

post 3–5 videos per day. study what gets views. double down on those formats.

don't overthink content quality. at this stage, volume > perfection.

a mediocre video that gets posted beats a "perfect" video sitting in your drafts.

WEEK 3–4: FIRST MONEY

by now you should have 40–80+ videos live across 2 platforms.

some will flop. some will hit 10K–100K+ views.

first affiliate commissions start hitting. maybe $5. maybe $50. maybe $113 like one of my students in his first few days.

the number doesn't matter yet. what matters is you proved the model works for YOU.

MONTH 2–3: COMPOUND

add a second channel. different niche or sub-niche.

same workflow. same tools. same monetisation stack.

you now have a content NETWORK, not a single channel.

scale to 5–8 videos per day total across channels.

$50–$100/day becomes realistic here.

MONTH 4–6: REAL MONEY

multiple channels. daily content. compounding views and affiliate earnings.

$100–$300/day. $3,000–$10,000/month.

from content you don't appear in. on platforms you barely check.

THE PART EVERYONE SKIPS:

90% of people who read this will save it and never open it again.

9% will start and quit in week 2 when they have 300 total views.

1% will post consistently for 90 days and wonder why they didn't start sooner.

which one are you?

comment "ROADMAP" and i'll send you the free guide to get started

(must be following)

cases.title:
Rant incoming... but this stuff right here is why we decided to leave Twitch.

A few months ago we made the permanent decision to leave Twitch due to the fact that we felt both the monetization and ad
cases.content:
Rant incoming... but this stuff right here is why we decided to leave Twitch.

A few months ago we made the permanent decision to leave Twitch due to the fact that we felt both the monetization and ad share revenue splits weren't worth the time and energy it took to put on a good stream. 

Streaming takes an insane amount of mental energy to do well and a lot of creators spend countless hours off stream to improve their on-stream appearance, persona, production value, etc. We in particular opted in for an engaging and interactive style of stream that, yes, when we maintained a continuous schedule, did quite well. However, it came at the great mental cost of always wondering whether people would show up, if the stream would be busy enough to keep us engaged, how new comers felt about the stream and our content from the get go, it was an endless list of worries that ultimately led us down the path of not wanting to stream at all.

We did end up taking an extended break and nearly quit producing content altogether. We're parents of 3 here at the Fort and content was a passion project, we weren't convinced that streaming had any long term value due to the fact that if you stop streaming, you fall off the face of the earth. We hated that feeling and it kept us from coming back.

That being said, we'd always loved posting Offline content and saw a few of our friends dedicating themselves to that grind and although growth was slow, it was there. 

This leads us into saying... yes. YouTube is harder to build up to monetization. They want to ensure you're determined, so they deter people by throwing up a big fat, "You need 1000 Subscribers and 4k Watch hours to make money", to keep out people that aren't heavily dedicated to their content. 

Here's the irony though... in a bit over 4 months we're nearly halfway to YouTube partner. In 2 years of being on Twitch, we never felt like we were "halfway" to being partner, even when our viewership average spiked into the mid 30s. In fact, we felt like we were still worlds away from making enough money on Twitch to consider making it a full-time gig, no matter how much we networked, grew our following, and produced content. Yet... in 4 months on YouTube we genuinely feel like we're half-way to a point where the channel would make enough money to render it worth our time and energy on a daily basis. Twitch... never made us feel that way.

Yes, some people do make it on Twitch and its through tons of hard work, mental stress, grinding, gaining friends, and losing them too. It's a Platform that's proven many times that it's not conducive to new streamers and in many cases, has sought to cut into their streamers income even deeper. I want you to consider too, that we've known a few Twitch Partners that work full-time jobs because streaming was too inconsistent to gamble on. 

This... dear Friends of our little Fort, is why we chose YouTube... and before you can say, "but I'll never get discovered as a streamer on there," we've gained a YouTube subscriber (one stream we even gained 3) every stream for the last 4 streams. Yeah, that might not seem like much, but we'd go multiple Twitch streams without ever gaining a follower. In fact, most of our Follows came from Raids, or Offline networking, both of which required us to put in work to meet people and hope they liked us enough to come visit our Content. Our Subscribers now come passively from videos that we spend a set amount of time editing and producing, and then they're permanently out there in the world, doing their thing and earning us Subscribers while we eat popcorn and snacks during moving time with the kids. The mental stress of YouTube is less than half that of Twitch.

Now... you can argue that YouTube is similar to Twitch if you're just streaming, however, we had someone come to our Stream that had been recommended our Stream from their YouTube feed. They'd apparently seen one of our funny Gang Beasts shorts earlier that day(completely unrelated to our review content), and when they saw the stream pop up, decided to stop in and say hi. They'd hit that big red Subscribe button half-way through the stream and hung out for a few hours. They still follow our content and engage with us... Twitch simply can't do that for you, which is yet another reason YouTube is where we went. Twitch can tell your followers to pay and promote you enough (crazy hype trains) to get you on the cover of a few peoples pages, but YouTube does it for free. Heck, YouTube even offers subscriptions for after you're partnered too, so not only do you make awesome ad revenue, your fans can still support you directly on YouTube.

Don't get us wrong, this isn't us attempting to sway you to a different platform, this is us stating why we chose to leave Twitch and this is yet another example of why we're glad we did. We know YouTube is harder... but once it hits returns, the returns are so much more consistent and reliable than just a raw streaming platform and it's why we'll rant about how glad we are that we switched all day. If you're considering it, try streaming there a few days over the next few weeks and see how it goes. Upload a gaming short of yours and see how well it does. It might just shock you 👀

cases.title:
The State of the Creator Economy

Assessing the Economic, Cultural, and Societal Impact of YouTube in the US in 2022

YouTube's creative ecosystem contributed over $35 billion to US GDP and supported
cases.content:
The State of the Creator Economy

Assessing the Economic, Cultural, and Societal Impact of YouTube in the US in 2022

YouTube's creative ecosystem contributed over $35 billion to US GDP and supported more than 390,000 FTE jobs in 2022.

Over 55,000 channels had more than 100,000 subscribers, and 6,500 channels had more than 1 million subscribers as of December 2022.

By sharing their interests, YouTube enables creators to find new opportunities, earn income, and launch new businesses.

Companies can use YouTube as a medium for connecting with audiences, building their brand, and training staff to increase revenue.

Users of all backgrounds and viewpoints can build communities around shared values and interests through the platform.

YouTube provides an educational resource for learners to gain skills and knowledge; parents/teachers recognize its educational value.

YouTube is the leading video-sharing platform worldwide, with more than 500 hours of content uploaded per minute.

Creators can earn money from its Ads and YouTube Premium subscriptions and through brand deals, ticket sales, fan funding, shopping, and channel memberships.

In the US alone, more than 430,000 creators and partners receive income linked to their YouTube presence, with 1 in 3 creators citing it as their primary source of revenue.

Over 40,000 channels earned money through alternative monetization products in June 2022 alone.

YouTube's income has both direct (GDP contribution and jobs), indirect (goods/services purchased), and induced (spending of wages in broader economy) economic impacts.

YouTube provides income and job opportunities for US creators, with over $35B in economic contribution and 390K+ full-time equivalent jobs.

83% of creators who earn from YouTube agree that the platform allows them to generate content and money they wouldn't get from traditional media.

69% of creators say revenue from advertisements is an essential source of income, while 74% report YouTube positively impacted their professional goals.

Dr. Tracey Marks leveraged YouTube to provide mental health education resources, leading to 1 million subscribers and a meaningful part of her overall income.

YouTube has enabled businesses to reach and build connections with new audiences, which can help generate sales revenue and growth.

83% of SMBs with a YouTube channel agree that YouTube played a role in helping them grow their customer base by reaching new audiences.

78% of SMBs who use YouTube agree that YouTube is essential to their business growth.

83% of businesses with a YouTube channel agree that YouTube has helped them develop a following in their local area.

80% of SMBs who use Youtube agree that providing staff training is a cost-effective way.

The Hercules Candy company experienced success after creating a YouTube channel; they received orders from around the US and abroad, re-opened a physical retail location after 70 years, bought new equipment, hired additional staff, and saw an overall increase in business.

YouTube enabled Nisha Vora to pursue her passion for cooking and grow her business quickly.

76% of users agree that YouTube is home to diverse content and perspectives, 72% of users value the diversity of content and perspectives found on YouTube, 81% of users hear from diverse communities and/or cultures from around the world, and 78% can easily find content in their preferred language.

21% of users have searched for political content on YouTube, with 82% finding it a source of diverse political viewpoints.

83% of female creators agree that YouTube helps them share their passions and ideas.

Encantos Media Studios uses YouTube to share Spanish language and cultural education through bilingual animated stories for kids called Canticos.

Canticos teaches early literacy, math concepts, songs, dance, and culture and has grown to over 420,000 subscribers with a global audience.

Dr. Javoris Hollingsworth launched Gracie's Corner on YouTube in 2020; the channel provides educational, fun, and encouraging songs featuring characters of diverse backgrounds and now has 1.5 million subscribers.

92% of users report using YouTube to gain information and knowledge, while 74% found it helpful during US elections by providing access to authoritative information.

77% of female users agree that YouTube is a platform for lifelong learning.

85% of parents who use YouTube/YouTube Kids agree it provides quality content.

86% of YouTube teachers have used its content in lessons and/or assignments.

77% of parents and teachers agree that YouTube (or YouTube Kids) helps kids learn and makes learning fun.

75% of parents agree that YouTube helps bridge education gaps.

In 2022, the total contribution of YouTube's creative ecosystem to the US GDP was over $35bn.

69% of creators who make money from advertisements on their channel reported it as an essential source of income.

390,000 FTE jobs were supported in the US in 2022 by YouTube's creative ecosystem.

74% of creators report a positive impact on their professional goals.

55,000 channels had >100k subscribers and 6,500 channels had >1m subscribers in December 2022.

83% of SMBs with a channel agree that it helps grow customer base & revenue; 78% consider it essential to business growth, and 80% find it a cost-effective way to provide staff training.

90% of creators want to use the influence they discovered on YouTube.

76% of YouTube users agree it is home to diverse content and perspectives.

81% agree that it provides access to different cultures worldwide.

78% report being able to find content in their preferred language.

72% value the diversity of content and perspectives on the platform.

92% of users use YouTube for information and knowledge gathering, and 77% of female users report using it for lifelong learning.

83% of female creators think that it helps them spread their ideas.

62% report being able to find local content on YouTube.

77% of parents believe that it (or YouTube Kids) helps their children learn, and 85% think it provides quality content for entertainment or educational purposes.

43% have looked for health information on the platform, with 72-77% finding reliable sources related to health or news topics, respectively.

75% of parents believe YouTube tackles gaps in education by providing access to quality information.

21% search for political content, 82% of whom find diverse viewpoints.

74% found it helpful during US elections for timely/authoritative info; 73% helps students learn; 85% provides quality content for entertainment/learning.

Businesses that utilize YouTube for advertising, a channel, or staff training are studied in the report.

Creators who upload videos to YouTube and those who receive revenue from YouTube are surveyed.

A Full-Time Equivalent (FTE) job is the amount of work equivalent to one person working full-time.

Media businesses, music businesses, and Small & Medium Businesses (SMBs) are included in the study.

Users viewing video content at least once a month on YouTube are also analyzed.

The Creative Ecosystem encompassing creators' employees, businesses, and freelancers in their supply chains that earn a substantial portion of their revenue from creators is studied.

Shorts is a platform where users can create short-form videos (60 seconds or less).

Oxford Economics surveyed over 4,000 YouTube users, 3,300 creators, and 600 businesses in Feb-Mar 2023.

Surveys were representative of the US population, with responses weighted to match the characteristics of YouTube's user base.

The survey also included collation and checking the response mix to ensure a good mix of creators with different-sized audiences.

Economic models include individuals or businesses with 10,000+ subscribers to the most extensive channel and those earning money directly from YouTube or other sources.

The total payout from YouTube was estimated using survey results and published information on music industry revenues; off-platform revenue was estimated from survey responses.

The direct GDP contribution of creative entrepreneurs is calculated by subtracting intermediate costs from associated revenue.

YouTube's impact report estimated the direct GDP contribution of music and media companies through a "production" approach.

An "input-output" model was used to estimate the supply chain (indirect) and worker spending (induced) impacts.

The study estimated full-time equivalent (FTE) jobs supported amongst creative entrepreneurs, their permanent employees, and through indirect and induced impacts.

Results exclude economic contributions from YouTube's operations and benefits businesses receive from advertising on YouTube.

Source : Google Survey Reports

cases.title:
Happy New Year everyone! Be safe tonight if you're going out to celebrate and do not drink and drive please 🙏

I want to take a second to look at my accomplishments and failures this year and what goa
cases.content:
Happy New Year everyone! Be safe tonight if you're going out to celebrate and do not drink and drive please 🙏

I want to take a second to look at my accomplishments and failures this year and what goals we met and didnt meet.

✅Get 100k followers on TikTok (we're at 102k now)
❌Get 20k subs on Main YT Channel (16.1k right now)
✅Start a Second Youtube Channel & get it Monetized
❌Get Partnered on Twitch
✅Start a Patreon and get 100 members (currently at 218 members)
✅Make over double what I did last year in Revenue and generate enough of a steady income to go full time if I wanted to.

I'm going to work my ass off for Youtube in 2024 and grow the both channels to hopefully hit 50k on both. I have a lot of plans for both channels next year. 

As for Twitch, I kind of stopped giving a shit about streaming overall and only do it when I feel like it or when there is a game that has a big content update that I am covering. I hope Palworld and Granblue Fantasy Relink change that for me, because as of right now I think the main problem is that there is nothing that I want to play on stream right now. I honestly don't like playing story games as much on stream anymore especially the first playthrough because people just love to foreshadow and spoil and backseat like crazy.

Thank you guys for an amazing year! I'm thankful to have met all of you! If you made it this far say something nice about meeting me this year or finding my content and I will do the same! ❤️

cases.title: Want to hear how I started YouTube Gaming as a forum that grew to over 20,000+ members, made a YouTube Network for a few months, sold the domain to YouTube, turned it into Damnlag; sold it and bought
cases.content:
Want to hear how I started YouTube Gaming as a forum that grew to over 20,000+ members, made a YouTube Network for a few months, sold the domain to YouTube, turned it into Damnlag; sold it and bought it back?

YouTube Gaming was first a community forum created in 2013 (I think) that was later named Damnlag, with over 20,000+ members. Yes, the domain https://t.co/htHvzx23Ni was once mine.

I sold the YouTubeGaming (dot) com Domain to YouTube. Well kinda... I sold it to a broker, who sold it to YouTube.

You remember Forums? I'm sure there's still plenty of popular forums today. They're not as popular as they once were. At least, from my experience with Damnlag.

So https://t.co/LBSOfKmqGm was at a time, a forum. A pretty large forum (for me), 20k members. It probably would have had 30k+ had I not made a huge error updating the forum software to another product early on. Still, 20,000 was a lot of people. I know there's others who are bigger, better, and more informed than I was.

The forum was designed to help small creators on YouTube grow and learn. It was called YouTube Gaming and ran on the domain https://t.co/IuNHZJVGkW for a while.

The forum had many different categories to promote  channels, content and live streams. As well as forums to find others to collaborate with on projects. Even a section to find others to game with. Lots of discussion sections for random and off-topic stuff. It was your ordinary forum for YouTubers. Later on, it included Twitch, Mixer and Facebook Gaming streamers.

How'd the forum get messed up?
At some point Xenforo either came out with a new forum software or was gaining popularity and I wanted to get away from vBulletin. So I decided to make the switch on forum software.

Between switching, something messed up and I lost all forum files. So I had to restart the forum from scratch.

The Growth
I'd like to say it grew pretty quickly from Twitter. 

I would reach out to many people on here and tell them about the forum and how they could promote their content. 

I'm sure I angered many people with my DM'ing strategy. But it worked for me. 

The YouTube Gaming Network
Yes, there was for a short time a YouTube network we had under the YouTube Gaming name. I had negotiated a deal with a network for 90/10 split and was one of the only ones doing a split like that. 

I wanted the creators to get damn near everything they were making. Of course the main network had to have a cut, so 10% was taken.

The network didn't last too long, 5 months I think to be exact. YouTube cut out the sub networks and we got dropped from everything.

The Domain Switch from YouTubeGaming to Damnlag
At the time, I was into making forums and buying domains, reselling domains. Sold a few sites and acquired a few. Nothing worth noting, however. 

When out of the blue, I was contacted by a broker about the https://t.co/htHvzx23Ni domain. Offered me a good chunk of money for it. 

Because I was sitting on some good domains, I decided to rebrand the community to https://t.co/LBSOfKmqGm and sell the YouTubegaming(com) domain to the broker. Unaware of who or why they wanted the domain to pay as much as they did, I said yes! and sold the domain.

A month or so later, YouTube announces the new platform which now sits on the https://t.co/8ShKrpMYX7 domain, YouTube Gaming. 

I was excited about how I had that domain and basically sold it to YouTube. And then I was also disappointed that I didn't counter the offer with something bigger. I was greedy, lol.

The new brand, Damnlag, grew into a decent little community. With over 20,000+ members at its peak. Not all were active users. Many of which would just register and promote their links/channels, before never coming back. So it was a big community, member wise. Just not active. 

Some stuck around, however. And we ended up creating a discord community with majority of them who joined it. That grew to over 2,000 members. But I wasn't as committed to 

Since the forum wasn't very active and full of people who just wanted to post a link to their channel/stream every so often, I grew apart from it. It didn't flourish and turn into the huge supportive community that I had hoped it to turn into. 

I expected it to be active, people helping others with issues related to everything YouTube related, gaming related, streaming, etc. 

I expected it to be a place for people to talk growth strategies and make new friends.

Some will probably tell you they made friends and some of the things we shared helped them, but not like I had hoped to.

I Sold Damnlag and Bought it Back
I sold the forum sometime just before the YouTube network got canned. It was making a few bucks with ads on the forum, affiliate income, and 5% on the network. Probably had 15,000 members at the time of sale.

When YouTube canned the sub networks, it hurt the community that was left. I ended up buying the forum back for a discounted price.

We grew the forum up and over 20,000 after buying it back. However, with a day job and family, I ended up losing the commitment to keeping it growing again.

Somewhere within the last year or so, I let the hosting expire and lost all forum files again. It was a dumb thing to do, but I was not active and paying much attention to my emails for a while.

The discord server sits at 2600+ members today. However, I can't access it as an administrator anymore due to my phone breaking and being replaced, without having my backup codes for 2FA. 

And that's how I created a large community, sold a domain to YouTube, ran a YouTube network, and tried to create a community to help small people grow on YouTube.

If this forum ever helped you gain a few views, subscribers or knowledge, share below. I'd love to hear how you've grown since.

cases.title: The claim of earning $9,000 per month on YouTube by November 2025 is highly dubious and likely unrealistic for most people. Successful monetization on YouTube requires significant effort, consistent c
cases.content:
The claim of earning $9,000 per month on YouTube by November 2025 is highly dubious and likely unrealistic for most people. Successful monetization on YouTube requires significant effort, consistent content creation, understanding of the platform's algorithms, and often a bit of luck with viral content. Earning $9,000 per month from YouTube typically depends on several factors, including view count, engagement, and ad revenue. Here's a detailed breakdown of the steps and challenges involved in monetizing YouTube:

1. **Creating Quality Content**:
   - **Consistency**: You need to consistently upload high-quality, engaging content that resonates with your audience. This requires a good camera, microphone, and video editing skills.
   - **Content Strategy**: Decide on your niche (e.g. tech reviews, vlogs, tutorials) and create a content plan. Keyword research helps in understanding what viewers are looking for.

2. **Building an Audience**:
   - **Engagement**: Engage with your audience through comments and social media. Encourage viewers to like, share, and subscribe.
   - **SEO for YouTube**: Use relevant keywords in your video title, description, and tags. Create eye-catching thumbnails and compelling titles to increase click-through rates.
   - **Collaborations**: Collaborate with other YouTubers to reach a wider audience. Coordinated promotions are another effective growth hack.

3. **Monetization**:
   - **Meet YouTube Partner Program (YPP) Requirements**: To start earning money, you need at least 1,000 subscribers and 4,000 watch hours in the past 12 months. Once approved, you can make money through ads, channel memberships, merchandise shelf, Super Chat & Super Stickers, and YouTube Premium revenue.
   - **Ad Revenue**: The amount you earn from ads depends on the number of views and the types of ads shown. CPM (cost per mile) rates can vary greatly, often ranging from $0.25 to $4 per 1,000 views.
   - **Sponsored Content**: Once you have a substantial following, brands may pay you to promote their products. This requires building a strong relationship with your audience and delivering authentic reviews or mentions of products you genuinely use.
   - **Affiliate Marketing**: Promote products using affiliate links in your video description. Earn a commission for any sales made through your link.

4. **Diversifying Income**:
   - **Patreon and Other Subscriptions**: Platforms like Patreon allow fans to support you directly. Offer exclusive content in return.
   - **Merchandise**: Sell branded merchandise through your website or YouTube’s merchandise shelf feature.

5. **Analytics and Adaptation**:
   - Continuously analyze your YouTube Analytics to understand what works and what doesn’t. Be ready to adapt your content strategy based on viewer feedback and trends.

Realistically, reaching $9,000 per month within a year is challenging. Very few YouTubers achieve this kind of success so quickly. The vast majority need multiple revenue streams and several years to grow their channels and income.

For leaderboard position alone, think of it like running a marathon vs running a 50 meter sprint. Monetize by engaging the community continuously. Consistency, experimentation, patience, insightful analytics are all key. People always overestimate what they can accomplish in a year and underestimate what they can do in ten years.

cases.title:
🔷 The 'YouTube Moment' of AI Data: IRYS and OpenLedger Join Forces

📌 The Power of Monetization through YouTube – and the Imbalance in AI

Before YouTube, monetizing user-generated video content was e
cases.content:
🔷 The 'YouTube Moment' of AI Data: IRYS and OpenLedger Join Forces

📌 The Power of Monetization through YouTube – and the Imbalance in AI

Before YouTube, monetizing user-generated video content was extremely difficult. That changed with the launch of the YouTube Partner Program in 2007. Creators began receiving a share of ad revenue, and between 2021 and 2023, YouTube paid over $7 billion to creators. In 2023, top YouTuber MrBeast reportedly earned more than $80 million, proving that YouTube had built a thriving creator economy where individuals, not just media giants, could succeed.

Meanwhile, the AI industry still lacks a similar value-sharing model. According to @Ramkumartweet, a core contributor at OpenLedger, “Today’s AI is an extractive economy that profits from invisible labor and centralized training pipelines.” Massive AI models derive value from publicly available data, yet the original contributors of that data rarely receive any compensation. To fix this imbalance, we need an AI ecosystem where individual contributors are fairly rewarded—just as YouTube did for creators.

📌 Technological Roles of IRYS and OpenLedger

This partnership brings together @irys_xyz and @OpenledgerHQ, each offering unique technologies to solve this issue:

🔹 IRYS – Onchain Data Infrastructure: IRYS is the world’s first programmable datachain—a Layer 1 blockchain designed for permanent, onchain storage of large-scale data with real-time processing capabilities. It merges storage and compute to provide an infrastructure optimized for AI development, enabling low-cost access to rich data streams onchain. Simply put, IRYS reliably fuels AI with trustworthy data.

🔹 OpenLedger – The Payable AI Platform: OpenLedger is a blockchain network supporting the monetization of AI development and data contribution. At its core is the idea of "Payable AI"—just like YouTube shares ad revenue with creators, OpenLedger distributes AI service revenue to data contributors, model developers, and more. This is made possible through Proof of Attribution, a mechanism that tracks and records how data contributes to model performance. Every time a model inference occurs, contributors are automatically rewarded via micropayments. OpenLedger enables transparent attribution throughout the AI lifecycle, ensuring that all participants receive their fair share.

📌 Expected Outcomes from the Partnership

The collaboration between IRYS and OpenLedger is expected to drive positive change throughout the AI ecosystem. According to the official announcement, IRYS’s structured and high-quality data sets will be integrated into OpenLedger’s AI pipeline, allowing for more precise fine-tuning and deployment of domain-specific AI models. Here's what this partnership unlocks:

1️⃣ Fair Rewards for Data Contributors: Individuals or organizations providing data to train AI models can now be automatically compensated every time their data is used. Rather than one-time contributions, data providers can earn ongoing revenue, empowering them and encouraging broader participation.

2️⃣ More Efficient AI Model Development: OpenLedger’s ModelFactory platform enables seamless fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs) using approved IRYS datasets. With no-code GUI access for data approval, model selection, training, evaluation, and deployment, development time is significantly reduced. LoRA and other efficient tuning techniques further accelerate the process, giving startups and researchers rapid access to build specialized AI models.

3️⃣ Trusted, High-Quality Data: Thanks to IRYS’s onchain storage and OpenLedger’s attribution system, AI models are trained on verifiable and transparent data. Every data origin is immutably recorded onchain. With quality-based incentives, contributors are encouraged to provide accurate and valuable data. OpenLedger’s model outputs also cite sources, enhancing AI explainability and trust.

4️⃣ A Virtuous Cycle for AI Ecosystems: In a “Payable AI” framework, a self-sustaining cycle emerges—data → models → users → rewards → more data. The more valuable the data, the better the models; the better the models, the higher the demand; the higher the usage, the more rewards distributed. This positive feedback loop drives sustainable and equitable growth in the AI industry.

📌 A New Wave in the Data Economy

Just as YouTube opened the door to countless creators around the world, the IRYS–OpenLedger partnership signals a new wave for the AI data economy. As the OpenLedger team puts it, this is AI’s "YouTube Moment"—a step toward recognizing the value of data and allowing individuals to generate sustained income from their contributions.

With this partnership, AI model development becomes more open, inclusive, and participatory. A new decentralized AI ecosystem is emerging—one where data providers, developers, and users all share in the value they help create. As IRYS and OpenLedger lead this shift, the future of a fair, transparent data economy in the age of AI looks brighter than ever.

@josh_benaron @lew_xyz

cases.content:
Monetizing Videos through NFT Sales and Royalties on Joystream Network

The @JoystreamDAO network offers a novel approach to video monetization by leveraging Non-Fungible Tokens (NFTs). Content creators can transform their videos into unique NFTs, minting them on the #Joystream blockchain. 

These NFTs can then be listed for sale, providing creators with a new revenue stream.

✓ Flexible Sales Options

Creators can choose from various sales options to suit their preferences:

1️⃣ Fixed Price: Set a fixed price for the NFT, allowing buyers to purchase it immediately.
2️⃣ Open Auction: Host an open auction, where buyers can bid on the NFT, driving up the price.
3️⃣ Timed Auction: Set a time limit for the auction, creating a sense of urgency among bidders.

✓ Royalty Payments

To ensure creators benefit from the long-term value of their content, the Joystream network implements a royalty system. Every time an NFT is resold within the network, the original creator receives a percentage of the sale price. This passive income stream incentivizes creators to produce high-quality content, knowing they'll benefit from its future success.

By combining NFT sales with royalty payments, the Joystream network provides a robust monetization framework for video creators. 

This innovative approach empowers creators to take control of their content's value, unlocking new revenue opportunities and promoting a thriving digital marketplace.

cases.title:
10 Legit Ways to Make More Money in Nigeria 🇳🇬 (practical, not “get rich quick”):

1. Learn a High-Income Skill

Digital skills like video editing, graphic design, copywriting, social media management
cases.content:
10 Legit Ways to Make More Money in Nigeria 🇳🇬 (practical, not “get rich quick”):

1. Learn a High-Income Skill

Digital skills like video editing, graphic design, copywriting, social media management, or coding can earn you in dollars if you work with foreign clients.

2. Start a Small Reselling Business

Buy items in bulk (clothes, perfumes, gadgets, thrift, foodstuff) and resell with a profit margin. Many people start with as little as ₦20k–₦50k.

3. Offer a Service in Your Area

Barbing, makeup, tailoring, phone repairs, POS services, laundry, cleaning — services are always in demand.

4. Monetize Social Media

Grow a niche page (motivation, prayer, finance, comedy, fashion). Earn through ads, brand deals, affiliate marketing, or digital products.

5. Sell Digital Products

Ebooks, online courses, templates, prayer guides, Canva designs — create once, sell repeatedly.

6. Learn Basic Investing

Start small with safe options like treasury bills, mutual funds, or reliable cooperative savings. Avoid “double your money” scams.

7. Freelance Online

Use platforms like Fiverr or Upwork to offer writing, design, voiceovers, or virtual assistant services.

8. Start a Side Hustle Alongside Your Job

Don’t depend on one salary. Even a weekend hustle (baking, mini importation, tutoring) adds extra income.

9. Improve Your Network

Opportunities in Nigeria often come from relationships. Attend events, engage with people's contents, and connect intentionally.

10. Solve a Local Problem

Look around you. What do people complain about? Water supply? Delivery delays? Long queues? Money follows problem-solvers.

Focus on consistency over speed. Many people quit too early. The real money usually comes after months of steady effort.

Ramesh had always been the quiet strength of his small family. After 35 years in a factory, his body ached, but his heart held dreams for daughter Priya’s bright future. When the factory shut down, his tiny pension struggled to cover Sunita’s medicines. Bills mounted, sleep vanished, and Priya’s college hopes dimmed.

One rainy day, an old friend spoke of the Post Office Monthly Income Scheme. With trembling hope, Ramesh took their hard-earned savings of ₹9 lakhs to the post office. The officer explained calmly: 7.4% interest, monthly payouts, principal safe, government guarantee.

He invested. The first cheque—₹5,550—arrived like a gentle blessing. Sunita smiled again. “No more worry for medicines,” she said softly. Priya studied without guilt. Month after month, the money came steadily—covering groceries, fees, treatments.

Ramesh felt useful once more. He told neighbours, “This isn’t just a scheme; it’s our new hope.” Evenings found him and Sunita on their small balcony, watching sunsets in peace.

Years later, Priya graduated and landed her first job. She hugged him tight. “Papa, you never gave up.” He smiled quietly. “Some things never fail, beta—like this Post Office Monthly Income Scheme.”

Even now, that monthly amount arrives faithfully—a tender reminder that wise choices and patience can carry a family through any storm.

cases.title:
I was scrolling through TikTok yesterday and came across a video of a girl sharing her story.

She worked a regular nine-to-five and earned about one million naira every single month. 

She was carefu
cases.content:
I was scrolling through TikTok yesterday and came across a video of a girl sharing her story.

She worked a regular nine-to-five and earned about one million naira every single month. 

She was careful with money and saved consistently. 

You know when you are young, have fewer responsibilities, earn well, and also have sense, saving feels like the obvious thing to do.

One day, she got to work and was fired.

In her mind, she thought, “I’ve been saving, so I should be able to manage for a few months.” She went home, stayed calm, and believed she would get another job within three months at most.

Three months passed. Then six months. Then it was almost a year.

Her savings finished.

That was when reality hit her hard. She talked about how difficult things became and how traumatized she felt.

Guys, relying on one source of income is risky. If you have a nine-to-five, try to build a side hustle.

Even if that side hustle brings in an extra ₦200k to ₦500k a month, it can make a huge difference when life happens.

Think about it this way. If your income stops in the next six months or one year, how will you survive?

At this point, it does not matter if you are a freelancer, a nine-to-fiver, or anything else. Everyone needs a backup.

I started my own side hustle earlier this year, and it is growing slowly alongside my freelance business.

And if you are a freelancer, you already know that working with just one client is bad business. Always keep your pipeline open.

Don't put all your eggs in one basket.

Happy weekend, my guys. What are you doing this weekend?

cases.title:
@AbstractChain’s live streaming feature has the potential to create a new meta for Web3 creators, but its success depends on adoption, incentives, and differentiation from Web2 streaming giants.

🚀 Bu
cases.content:
@AbstractChain’s live streaming feature has the potential to create a new meta for Web3 creators, but its success depends on adoption, incentives, and differentiation from Web2 streaming giants.

🚀 Bull Case: A Game-Changer for Web3 Content
On-Chain Monetization 🎥💰Native crypto tipping, NFT-gated streams, and tokenized subscriptions could offer creators better monetization than Web2 platforms that take heavy cuts.
If integrated with DeFi yield strategies, creators could earn passively from their stream revenues.

Decentralization = No Censorship 🔓Unlike YouTube and Twitch, a decentralized streaming platform means no bans, demonetization, or algorithmic suppression.
Content creators in controversial niches (crypto trading, politics, independent media, etc.) might flock to it.

New Interactive Creator Economy 🎭Web3-native features like NFT-based access, DAO-driven content curation, and live token rewards could make streams more immersive than Web2.
Viewers could have governance power over streamers they support, influencing content direction.

⚠️ Bear Case: Struggling for Adoption
Web2 Giants Still Dominate 🏢YouTube, Twitch, and Kick already own the audience—getting creators to leave stable income for Web3 won't be easy.
Most casual users don’t care about decentralization; they just want quality content and smooth UX.

Scalability & UX Issues ⏳Decentralized video streaming faces major latency and bandwidth challenges compared to Web2 CDNs.
If streams are laggy or expensive, viewers won’t switch.

Token Incentives Could Lead to Farm & Dump Behavior 📉If the platform relies too much on token rewards, it might attract farming bots instead of real engagement.
Creators cashing out rewards instead of building genuine communities could weaken long-term sustainability.

Final Take: The Meta Shift Depends on Execution
If AbstractChain can combine seamless UX, strong token incentives, and unique Web3-native features, it could attract a niche audience and gradually challenge Web2 streaming. But if the UX is clunky or incentives aren’t aligned, it might struggle to break out.

Key to success? Major creator partnerships, smooth streaming tech, and real Web3-native engagement tools. 🚀

cases.title:
Yesterday I spent over 7 hours researching about inauthentic wave, diverse niche analysis and the future of YTA, here’s my piece:

In as much as we all hate the wave, we must learn to accept the truth
cases.content:
Yesterday I spent over 7 hours researching about inauthentic wave, diverse niche analysis and the future of YTA, here’s my piece:

In as much as we all hate the wave, we must learn to accept the truth, it’s going to get worse from here, not because YT hates us but because this is indirectly the right thing to do, don’t curse me yet, I’ve been hit too and I’ve lost revenue so I understand too. 

If you study the YPP revenue anatomy you’ll realize they’re doing this to protect the company’s reputation, creators and revenue. I’ve been a Google/YouTube advertiser for over 4 years so I understand from both a creator and an advertiser perspective. 

The money we’re all looking for as creators is powered by advertisers and without YT deploying this policy in 2025, we will both suffer it, the repetition and low production in content is damaging the company’s audience trust, purchase power etc, and this damage affects advertisers which might force them to deviate to better alternative platforms like meta which automatically affects both YouTube and YPP creators, because when there is no advertiser to spend they will be no ad slots on your content, I believe you know what that means and you now have a grasp of how this works.

The only way the company could strive is by filtering out lazy creators who’s content damages their audience trust and behavior, yesterday I saw 6 different channels using the same title, thumbnail and script pattern with just slight difference, as a viewer if I keep getting those on my homepage with multitude of ads popped together I’ll definitely exit the app, not because I don’t enjoy storytelling, documentary or educational content but because there’s no real value or channel (creators) anymore.

If these keeps going on, it will damage users trust, which indirectly affects advertisers and creators. AI was meant to empower efficiency, boost productivity and not the way most of us are using it, Google/YT adopted Ai in their platform too and that shows you there are not against the use of Ai in their platform.

This inauthentic rule is a bridge against lazy creators, which indirectly favors you, but you can only see the advantage when you stop hating and start adapting fast.

While doing research yesterday both manually and with Claude Ai, I figured out a lot and one of thing I’ll tell you is if you’re still in the game of remodeling competitors then there’s no future for you in YTA. 

Remodeling I mean is template uses, rewriting transcripts with different angle using ai or any form you might think of can’t safe you, tweaking titles and thumbnails can’t either, the system that sync these things sync patterns and that’s why any day there’s a big wave (ban) it affects dozens of creators because of same pattern of mass production.

You’re called a creator for a purpose, act like one, evolve with ai and don’t be a slave to it, YouTube will always reward creativity till the end time, so how creative are you?

In my next long form tweet like this I’ll talk about the do’s and don’t you need to know in content production, Ai is here to make us more creative with endless ideas, however most only use it as a copy and paste tool.

Until we meet again, Cheers🥂
